faq.a: Aenaes tracks the location of cargo containers on a global scale using a 1 Watt Wi-Fi-like transceiver. faq.a: Alba Cluster 3 is the largest PocketQube launch in history to date. It builds on the company's successful Alba Cluster 2 launch in 2019, which deployed six PocketQubes into orbit, including two of Alba Orbital's Unicorn-2 pico-satellites. Alba Cluster 3 was launched using the company's AlbaPod v2, which allows PocketQubes to be integrated into any launch vehicle as part of a ‘rideshare' mission.
faq.q: How does Alba Cluster 3 differ from other Alba Cluster missions?

faq.a: Alba Orbital (UK, USA, Germany) is making space access easier, faster and more affordable for small organisations. Alba's January SpaceX flight alone broke records as the world's largest PocketQube launch to date with 13 satellites manifested on board. Future rideshare plans with Alba AMSAT-EA (Spain) have already been announced.
faq.q: What records have Alba Orbital broken?

faq.a: PocketQubes are highly miniaturised satellites, one eighth the size of a CubeSat, consisting of one or more cubic units of 5 cm with a maximum mass of 250 g per unit or ‘P'. This reduces the costs and development time involved with satellite development, widening the doors of space access for small organisations to develop their own space program.
faq.q: How is Alba Orbital democratising access to space?

faq.a: ALOS only had one paddle to ensure that the mission sensor onboard could secure a broader view, but there was no alternative source of power if that paddle was broken. ALOS-2’s radar pointed towards the nadir direction (directly towards Earth), so a second paddle would not obstruct its view. Thus, a second paddle was installed to ensure a backup source of power if one paddle is damaged.
faq.q: Why does ALOS-2 have two solar array paddles, but ALOS only had one?

faq.a: Radio waves have the benefit of being able to be used day and night as it does not rely on sunlight being reflected from Earth. L-band radio waves are special due to their longer wavelength of 15 - 30 cm, which allows them to penetrate more objects than shorter-wavelength radio waves. They can penetrate rain and clouds allowing ALOS-2 to operate regardless of weather, and can also penetrate forest canopies. The ability to penetrate vegetation is useful for studying land deformations which are prevalent in earthquake-prone Japan.
faq.q: Why does the imaging radar on ALOS-2 use L-band radio waves?

faq.a: After separating from the launch vehicle, ALOS-2 entered Rate Damping Mode in which the rate at which it was spinning was slowed using thrusters. Sun Acquisition Mode then began in which the solar array paddles extended and the satellite orientated itself such that the paddles faced the sun. ALOS-2 then turned to face the Earth in Earth Acquisition Mode. Following this, the L-band SAR antenna and transmission antenna was deployed before Fine Normal Mode, in which the equipment and orbit were verified for operation.
faq.q: What steps were taken from the launch of ALOS-2 for it to be operational?

faq.a: A Molniya orbit is a highly eccentric orbit (HEO) with an inclination of 63.4° and an argument of perigee of 270°. It has an apogee of 39750 km, a perigee of 1043 km, and a period of half a sidereal day (11 hours and 58 minutes). It’s able to monitor polar regions for extended periods of time which is extremely useful due to the inability of satellites to maintain a geostationary orbit over the polar regions.
faq.q: What is a Molniya orbit?

faq.a: The Artemis program was developed to return humans to the Moon, support sustained exploration on and around the Moon, and build experience and capabilities for future human missions to Mars. Before later landing missions, NASA is using Artemis flights to test the Orion spacecraft, the Space Launch System, and commercially developed Human Landing Systems.
faq.q: Why was the Artemis Program developed?

faq.a: NASA has five currently planned Artemis missions, Artemis I through V, with six additional missions proposed.
faq.q: How many Artemis missions are planned?

faq.a: The Artemis Base Camp is being developed to allow astronauts to stay on the Moon’s surface for more than a month at a time, enabling them to collect and analyse minerals and resources there.
faq.q: How will the astronauts be able to live on the Moon?

detail.name: Artemis Program
detail.summary: It aims to establish a sustained human presence on the Moon and build a foundation for long-term exploration beyond Earth.

faq.a: The Arctic Satellite Broadband Mission (ASBM) is owned by Space Norway, operated by the state-owned company’s subsidiary HEOSAT and the US Space Force, and developed by the company Northrop Grumman.
faq.q: Who owns the Arctic Satellite Broadband Mission?

faq.a: The Arctic Satellite Broadband Mission (ASBM) aims to provide broadband coverage to the Arctic, closing a connectivity gap. The mission will fulfil an emerging commercial and strategic interest to the Arctic driven by retreating polar ice, which requires the development of crucial infrastructure like mobile broadband.
faq.q: What is the purpose of the Arctic Satellite Broadband Mission?

faq.a: The ASBM satellites carry radio communication payloads, each of them with a Ka-band Global Xpress 10 from Viasat, an X-band radio communications instrument for the Norwegian Ministry of Defence, and the Enhanced Polar Systems-Recapitalization (EPS-R) instrument from the US Space Force. ASBM-1 will also carry the Norwegian Radiation Monitor (NORM).
faq.q: What instruments does the Arctic Satellite Broadband Mission carry?

detail.name: ASBM (Arctic Satellite Broadband Mission)
detail.summary: The mission is jointly operated by Space Norway’s subsidiary HEOSAT, Viasat, and the US Space Force (USSF), with the satellites developed by Northrop Grumman.

faq.a: ASNARO was launched in November 2014, on board a Dnepr-1 launch vehicle from Dombarovsky in Russia.

faq.a: The Afternoon-train (A-train) is a constellation of satellites that closely follow one another along the same, or very similar, orbital track. The original constellation consisted of CloudSat, CALIPSO, Aqua, Aura, and Parasol with GCOM-W1 and OCO-2 joining later. Cloudsat exited the A-train following the loss of one of its reaction wheels in 2018 and it was decided that CALIPSO would also change its orbit to join CloudSat in the new C-train.
faq.q: What is the A-train?

faq.a: Aura, which is owned and operated by the National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA), has the overall objective of studying the chemistry and dynamics of Earth’s atmosphere from the ground through the mesosphere.
faq.q: What is the aim of Aura?

detail.name: Aura (EOS/Chem-1)
detail.summary: Launched in July 2004, Aura is the third mission in the Earth Observing Satellite (EOS) series, following on from Terra and Aqua.

faq.a: The purpose of the Balkan Constellation is to provide high-resolution multispectral Earth observation imagery to support applications in land use and land cover mapping, emergency management, coastal and maritime monitoring, and security services, including those delivered through the Copernicus Programme.
faq.q: What is the purpose of the Balkan Constellation?

faq.a: The Balkan Constellation is planned as a multi-satellite system comprising up to 120 satellites deployed in coordinated orbital planes to increase revisit frequency.
faq.q: How many satellites will the Balkan Constellation include?

faq.a: BeaverCube is an educational mission led by MIT (Massachusetts Institute of Technology) to introduce students worldwide to aerospace science and technology. Data collected by BeaverCube has many applications, including improving the understanding of regional biological productivity and the effects of extreme weather events. BeaverCube also demonstrates the use of low-cost commercial cameras to obtain measurements and demonstrates the operation of Tiled Ionic Liquid Electrospray (TILE) propulsion technology.
faq.q: What is the purpose of BeaverCube?

faq.a: BeaverCube hosts two Long-Wave Infrared (LWIR) cameras and one Visible Spectrum (VIS) camera. These instruments measure ocean colour as well as sea surface and cloud top temperature. This data has many applications in the scientific study of Earth’s climate and weather.
faq.q: What does BeaverCube measure?

faq.a: On 03 June, 2021, the BeaverCube 3U CubeSat was launched as a payload on the SpaceX CRS-22 cargo mission from the Kennedy Space Center in Florida.
faq.q: When was BeaverCube launched?

detail.name: BeaverCube CubeSat Mission
detail.summary: Launched in June 2021, BeaverCube measures VIS (visible) and LWIR (long wave infrared) spectral data to obtain colour and thermal images of the ocean surface and cloud tops, which have many applications including monitoring regional biological productivity and the effects of extreme weather events.

faq.a: There are two satellites in the Beijing-3 constellation, Beijing-3A and -3B.

faq.a: Biomass will address a fundamental gap in our understanding of terrestrial Earth - the status and dynamics of Earth’s forests. The mission aims to take measurements of forest biomass to assess terrestrial carbon stocks and fluxes for a better understanding of the carbon cycle.
faq.q: What is the purpose of the Biomass satellite mission?

faq.a: Biomass carries a P-band Synthetic Aperture Radar (P-SAR) onboard alongside its corresponding P-band Antenna Feed Subsystem.
faq.q: What instruments are onboard the Biomass satellite mission?

detail.name: Biomass (Earth Explorer 7)
detail.summary: Launched in April 2025 and led by the European Space Agency (ESA) as the seventh Earth Explorer, Biomass addresses the status and dynamics of forests for a better understanding of the carbon cycle.

faq.a: BioSentinel’s flight path will initially take it past the moon and then subsequently into an Earth trailing, Heliocentric orbit.
faq.q: What is the flight trajectory of BioSentinel?

faq.a: BioSentinel is set to quantify the long term effects of space radiation on genetic material, allowing future missions to minimise the detrimental health impacts on humans in space.
faq.q: How will BioSentinel advance human deep space flight?

faq.a: The biosensor payload contains three forms of Saccharomyces cerevisiae (baker’s yeast), two controlled strains and one engineered strain.
faq.q: What type of cells are present in the BioSentinel biosensor?

detail.summary: BioSentinel was launched in November 2022 onboard the Artemis 1 mission.

faq.q: What is the design life of BIRD?

faq.a: BIRD data is used for measurements of vegetation, albedo and reflectance, surface temperature on land, and multi-purpose imagery.
faq.q: What is BIRD data used for?

faq.a: The BIRD satellite carried Imaging Multi-Spectral Radiometers in the visible and infrared bands.

faq.a: BIROS stands for Bi-spectral InfraRed Optical System.
faq.q: What does BIROS stand for?

faq.a: The primary goal of BIROS was the detection and monitoring of HTEs (High Temperature Events), e.g. forest fires or other hot spots.
faq.q: What was the primary goal of BIROS?

faq.a: BIROS was equipped with a new optical telecommunications terminal, OSIRIS, demonstrating downlink communication up to 1 Gbit/s. BIROS also tested the AVANTI software, a vision-based navigation architecture, demonstrating the rendezvous to a non-cooperative satellite, e.g. a malfunctioning satellite.
faq.q: What were the secondary goals of BIROS?

detail.name: BIROS (Bi-spectral InfraRed Optical System)
detail.summary: Launched in June 2016, BIROS served emergency response agencies internationally until its end of life in December 2020.

faq.a: The final planned BlackSky Constellation will consist of 60 microsatellites.
faq.q: How many BlackSky Constellation satellites are there?

faq.a: Data collected by the BlackSky Constellation is not free, but is commercially available through BlackSky Global, the owner and operator of the program.
faq.q: Is BlackSky Constellation data free?

faq.a: BlackSky Constellation program aims to provide high temporal and spatial resolution imaging of the Earth for purchase by corporations, governments and other entities.

detail.summary: The constellation aims to provide higher temporal resolution and lower cost Earth imaging, and currently contains 19 operational microsatellites, each with an expected lifetime of 4 years.

faq.a: Blue Moon will provide high-resolution thermal infrared imagery for climate monitoring, urban monitoring, agriculture, and security applications.

faq.q: Who is developing the Blue Moon satellite?

faq.a: The Blue Moon mission carries an uncooled micro-bolometer array telescope operating between 8 and 13 microns, with a 120 cm unfolding aperture and 3.3 m Ground Sample Distance (GSD).
faq.q: What payload does Blue Moon carry?

detail.name: Blue Moon
detail.summary: The mission will support applications in climate resilience, urban heat management, agriculture, and national security, while also demonstrating novel deployable telescope technology.

faq.a: The BlueBon satellite was developed by TelePIX, a Korean total satellite AI solution provider, in collaboration with Polish aerospace company SatRev. The AI-powered onboard processor, TetraPLEX, and the optical multispectral camera payload were developed by TelePIX, while the satellite bus was designed based on the SatRev ScopeSat nanosatellite platform.
faq.q: Which companies developed the BlueBon satellite?

faq.a: BlueBon was launched on January 14, 2025, from Vandenberg Space Force Base, in California, U.S, aboard a Falcon 9 rocket as part of the SpaceX Transporter-12 rideshare mission.
faq.q: When did the BlueBon satellite launch?

faq.a: BlueBon’s proprietary payload is a multispectral camera developed by TelePIX to verify the quantity and distribution of floating algae for applications in the carbon credit market and climate crisis responses.
faq.q: What is the primary payload aboard BlueBon?

detail.name: BlueBon
detail.summary: Launched on 14 January 2025, the satellite uses an AI-powered onboard processor and data analysis software to analyse blue carbon in optical imagery acquired from BlueBon’s multispectral camera.

detail.name: BRO (Breizh Reconnaissance Orbiter) / Unseenlabs
detail.summary: The constellation is designed to enhance Maritime Domain Awareness by delivering spectrum monitoring and electromagnetic intelligence (SIGINT) services for maritime traffic surveillance.

faq.a: The Earth Science Data Systems (ESDS) program provides open access to NASA’s collection of Earth observation data. The data can be accessed on the Earth Data website, free of charge.

faq.a: CALIPSO is the first  satellite to provide information on the vertical structure of clouds and aerosols, as well as provide vertically-resolved estimates of the amount of rain and ice in clouds. These observations are then used to make an assessment as to how much clouds and aerosols contribute to global warming.
faq.q: What makes CALIPSO unique?

faq.a: CALIPSO is also referred to as Earth System Science Pathfinder-3 (ESSP-3), as well as Pathfinder Instruments for Cloud and Aerosol Spaceborne Observations - Climatologie Etendue des Nuages et des Aérosols (PICASSO-CENA).

faq.a: CanX-2 had the primary goal to demonstrate several technologies critical for the CanX-4/5 formation flying mission, including a custom cold-gas propulsion system, a 30 mNms nanosatellite reaction wheel as part of a three-axis stabilised momentum-bias attitude control system, and a commercially available GPS receiver.
faq.q: What was the main purpose of CanX-2?

faq.a: CanX-4 and -5 are two satellites, utilised in 2014 to demonstrate formation flying using the nanosatellite scale technology that had been tested on CanX-2. These two satellites were launched together, commissioned together, and then separated in orbit.
faq.q: What are CanX-4 and -5?

faq.a: CartoSat-2 was able to provide specific spot images that were useful for cartographic applications, urban and rural infrastructure development and management, and various other Land Information System (LIS) and Geographical Information System (GIS) applications.

faq.a: The name CartoSat-2 comes from the concatenation of cartography (the science or practice of drawing maps) and satellite. CartoSat-2 is the second satellite of the CartoSat Series.

faq.a: CartoSat-2D provides regular, high resolution remote sensing imagery, which can be used for cartography, environmental monitoring, disaster relief, and event monitoring. For example, the satellite may be used in determining the status of water sources, which can allow effective planning for efficient usage of water.
faq.q: What is the use of CartoSat-2D?

faq.a: CartoSat-2D is operated by ISRO (Indian Space Research Organisation).
faq.q: Who operates CartoSat-2D?

faq.a: There are seven CartoSat-2 satellites: CartoSat 2, 2A, 2B, 2C, 2D, 2E, and 2F. CartoSat-2D is the fifth satellite in this series.
faq.q: How many CartoSat-2 satellites are there?

detail.name: CartoSat-2D
detail.summary: It is the fifth in a series of seven satellites of the CartoSat-2 series.

faq.a: CartoSat-2E is an Earth observation mission, aiming to design, develop, launch and operate an advanced optical remote sensing satellite for providing spot imagery with high spatial resolution, to be used in urban planning, infrastructure development, utilities planning and traffic management.
faq.q: What is the purpose of CartoSat-2E?

faq.a: CartoSat-2E was launched on 23 June 2017 on ISRO’S PSLV-C38 vehicle from the Satish Dhawan Space Center in India, along with 29 co-passenger satellites onboard.
faq.q: How was CartoSat-2E launched?

faq.a: CartoSat-2E has an orbital period of 97.4 minutes.
faq.q: What is the satellite's orbital period?

detail.name: CartoSat-2E
detail.summary: The mission is currently operational,  having been launched in June 2017 with the objective to collect high-resolution, large-scale imagery to be used in urban planning, infrastructure development, utilities planning and traffic management.

faq.a: Cartosat-3’s purpose is to provide imagery to support cartographic mapping, urban and rural planning, infrastructure development and disaster management. It is operated by ISRO and launched on the PSLV-C47 mission, supplying data primarily for national applications, with additional use in commercial mapping and monitoring applications.
faq.q: What is the purpose of Cartosat-3?

faq.a: There have been nine Cartosat satellites launched to date, beginning with Cartosat-1 in 2005 and followed by multiple Cartosat-2 series missions and Cartosat-3 in 2019. Further satellites in the Cartosat series are anticipated as part of ISRO’s Earth observation programme.
faq.q: How many Cartosat satellites are there?

faq.a: The PSLV-C47 was used to launch Cartosat-3 into orbit on 27 November 2019.
faq.q: What rocket was used for the launch of Cartosat-3?

detail.name: Cartosat-3 (Cartographic Satellite-3)
detail.summary: Launched in November 2019 aboard PSLV-C47, it supports national mapping and monitoring applications through advanced panchromatic and multispectral imaging.

faq.a: ISRO attempted to re-establish contact with the Vikram Lander and Pragyan Rover on the 22nd September 2023, but the harsh and cold lunar nights (lasting 14 Earth days) seems to have affected their batteries irreparably. On the 4th December 2023, ISRO tasked the Propulsion Module (PM) to move back into an Earth orbit with its fuel reserves to make additional observations in support of future lunar missions.

faq.a: CICERO GNSS-RO data is processed to create high-accuracy profiles of atmospheric pressure, temperature, and moisture; 3D maps of the electron distribution in the ionosphere; and ocean and ice properties. This data is used in weather forecasting, climate research, and space weather monitoring. The data is commercially available to government and business users and is free for research purposes. CICERO currently has a contract with NOAA (National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration) to provide daily radio occultation soundings.
faq.q: What is CICERO data used for?

faq.a: GeoOptics is an international consortium founded by working scientists. GeoOptics created the CICERO mission, following a community approach, to ensure continuing GNSS-RO observations. Their goal is to provide high-quality radio occultation data from space.
faq.q: What is GeoOptics?

faq.a: GNSS-RO stands for Global Navigation Satellite System Radio Occultation. GNSS-RO is a remote sensing technique in which low Earth-orbiting satellites measure the atmospheric refraction of radio signals sent by GNSS satellites. Atmospheric refractivity is a function of temperature, density, pressure, and water vapour content meaning these measurements can profile the Earth's atmosphere and ionosphere with high vertical resolution. GNSS-RO is the primary sensing method of CICERO satellites.
faq.q: What is GNSS-RO?

faq.a: Copernicus is the Earth observation component of the European Union's Space programme. It offers information services that draw from space-based and in-situ data.
faq.q: What is the Copernicus Programme?

faq.a: The EU Copernicus programme is co-funded by the European Union and ESA, with a minor contribution from EUMETSAT.
faq.q: Who funds the Copernicus programme?

faq.a: ESA is the European Union’s main partner and co-funder of the programme, and has the mandate to coordinate the space component, also acting as system architect, and serving users with satellite data available through the dedicated satellite missions, the Sentinel families, and through the Copernicus Contributing Missions.
faq.q: What’s ESA’s role on Copernicus?

faq.a: Copernicus primarily builds on the data collected from dedicated EO satellites built for the programme, the Sentinels, and complemented by other missions, the Copernican Contributing Missions, from which part of the data is used by Copernicus, all of them making up, along with an integrated ground infrastructure capable of delivering all the data produced to the users, the Copernicus Space Component.
faq.q: What is the Copernicus Space component?

faq.a: The Sentinel family of satellites form the backbone of the space component of the European Union’s Copernicus programme. They have been developed by ESA specifically to answer users’ needs and collect data on a range of Earth system parameters.
faq.q: What are the Sentinel satellites?

faq.a: The Copernicus Space Segment includes the six Sentinel missions of the first generation (Sentinel-1, 2, 3, 4 , 5P/5 and 6), as well as the six future Copernicus Expansion Missions (CHIME, CIMR, CO2M, CRISTAL, LSTM and ROSE-L), the Next Generation Sentinels (Sentinel-1 Next Gen, Sentinel-2 Next Gen, Sentinel-3 Next Gen Optical, Sentinel-3 Next Gen Topographic and Sentinel-6 Next Gen) and Copernicus Contributing Missions.
faq.q: How many Copernicus satellite missions are there?

detail.name: Copernicus Space Programme
detail.summary: The space component consists of the Sentinel missions, as well as the Copernicus Contributing Missions, and is supported by the Copernicus Ground Segment.

faq.a: The Sentinel data policy, established by the European Space Agency and European Commission, provides full and open access to the data. The data can be accessed on the Copernicus Hub, free of charge.
faq.q: Can I access Sentinel-1 data?

faq.a: Sentinel-1 satellites are one of the few Sentinel satellites to have an imaging microwave radar onboard, in order to suit the Sentinel-1 objectives. Whilst Sentinel-1 is focused on continuously providing radar mapping of land and sea, other Sentinel missions are concerned with optical observations of land and coastlines, air quality, and sea-surface topography and temperature.
faq.q: How does Sentinel-1 differ from other Sentinel missions?

faq.a: The COSMO-SkyMed mission is a 4 spacecraft constellation conceived by the ASI (Agenzia Spaziale Italiana) and funded by the Italian Ministry of Defence and the Italian Ministry of Research. It provides Earth observation data used for military, research and commercial purposes.
faq.q: What is the COSMO-SkyMed mission?

faq.a: COSMO-SkyMed satellites allow for multimodal acquisition. The ‘Spotlight’ operational mode has a spatial resolution of  less than 1m, whereas the ‘Stripmap’ mode has a spatial resolution of 3-15m. The two ScanSAR modes (Wideregion and Hugeregion) have a lower spatial resolution of 30m and 100m respectively.
faq.q: What is the resolution of COSMO-SkyMed spacecraft?

faq.a: The aim of the COSMO-SkyMed Second Generation is to enhance the capabilities of the first generation constellation. Its implementation grants greater operative versatility, imaging quality and performance capabilities, leading to a greater variety of user applications.
faq.q: Why was the second generation of the COSMO-SkyMed developed?

faq.a: The first satellite of the COSMO-SkyMed Second Generation was launched in December 2019 and the second satellite was launched in January 2022. The remaining two satellites are due to be launched in February 2024 and February 2025.

faq.a: COSPAS translates to Space System for the Search of Distressed Vessels, while SARSAT stands for Search and Rescue Satellite Aided Tracking System.
faq.q: What does COSPAS-SARSAT stand for?

faq.a: COSPAS-SARSAT is an intergovernmental and internationally operated series of satellites for the location of associated emergency beacons to aid in and initiate search and rescue operations.
faq.q: What does COSPAS-SARSAT do?

faq.q: What is CRISTAL for?

detail.name: CRISTAL (Copernicus Polar Ice and Snow Topography Altimeter)

faq.a: CryoSat was owned and operated by ESA, as the first Earth Explorer Opportunity Mission in its Living Planet Program.
faq.q: Who owned CryoSat?

faq.a: CryoSat was lost in a launch failure on 8 October 2005, prompting the development of a successor mission, CryoSat-2.
faq.q: What happened to CryoSat?

faq.a: The CryoSat sensor complement consisted of three instruments, the Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) Interferometer Radar Altimeter (SIRAL) (a Ku-band radar altimeter), the Doppler Orbitography and Radio Positioning Integration by Satellite (DORIS) instrument, which would have measured the Doppler frequency shifts of both VHF and S-Band signals transmitted by ground beacons, and a Laser Retroreflector (LRR) (a passive optical sensor to be used as a secondary method of orbit determination).
faq.q: What instruments did CryoSat carry?

detail.name: CryoSat (Earth Explorer Opportunity Mission-1)

faq.a: CryoSat-2 measures land and sea ice sheet thickness to help explain the connection between the melting of polar ice and the rise in sea levels.

faq.q: What were the launch details of CryoSat-2?

faq.a: ESA’s Earth Explorer Missions are a set of missions dedicated to addressing major challenges identified by society. Five missions have been or are in operation (GOCE, SMOS, CryoSat, Swarm, Aeolus) and five further missions are approved for development  (EarthCARE, Biomass, FLEX, FORUM, Harmony).
faq.q: What are ESA’s Earth Explorer Missions?

faq.a: The ionosphere exists in the upper radius of Earth’s atmosphere and interacts with the magnetosphere. It is named after the presence of ionised particles that result from solar radiation. The CSES mission studies the lithosphere-atmosphere-ionosphere pairing due to its under-exploited influence upon Earth’s seismic activities. Patterns of ionosphere/lithosphere disturbances have previously been observed during and after earthquake activity, and thus it is theorised that improved monitoring of the charged particles in these regions can aid in preparation and response to earthquakes.
faq.q: Why does the CSES mission study the ionosphere?

faq.a: CSES-02 was launched in June 2025 to complement the already present CSES-01, decreasing net revisit time and improving the quality of data gathered. With both satellites orbiting out of phase by 180 degrees, the constellation’s revisit time has been reduced from 5 to 2.5 days.
faq.q: Has the CSES-02 mission replaced the CSES-01 mission?

faq.a: CSES-01 houses additional instruments from CSES-01: the Medium Energy Electron Detector (MEED) and the Ionospheric Photometer (IP). MEED is a dedicated particle detector covering electrons, identifying their charge, orientation and particle type. The existing instruments also have improved accuracy and sensitivity to fluctuations of their respective measurement.
faq.q: What are the main differences between CSES-01 and CSES-02?

faq.a: GPS satellites reflect radio signals off the ocean surface towards CYGNSS satellites. When wind speeds are high, the ocean becomes rougher which scatters the signal and makes the received signal much weaker than the transmitted signal. The exact ocean surface wind speed is calculated from the received signal strength by the CYGNSS wind speed retrieval algorithm.
faq.q: How does CYGNSS measure ocean surface wind speed?

faq.a: Eight satellites make up CYGNSS. They follow the same orbit with each pair being separated by 12 minutes.
faq.q: How many satellites make up CYGNSS?

faq.a: The DQ-1 and DQ-2 satellites aim to monitor the Earth's atmospheric environment by measuring aerosols, clouds, and greenhouse gases such as carbon dioxide and methane. The missions provide data for studies of air quality, climate change, and atmospheric composition.
faq.q: What is the purpose of the DQ-1 and DQ-2 satellites?

faq.a: The DQ-1 satellite was launched on April 15, 2022, aboard a Long March 4C rocket from the Taiyuan Satellite Launch Centre.
faq.q: When was DQ-1 launched?

faq.a: The DQ-1 and DQ-2 satellites carry multiple instruments. DQ-1 carries the Aerosol and Carbon Detection Lidar (ACDL), Directional Polarisation Camera-2 (DPC-2), Particulate Observing Scanning Polarimeter (POSP), Environment Monitoring Instrument-2 (EMI-2), while DQ-2 will carry the Greenhouse-gases Absorption Spectrometer-2 (GAS-2) and ACDL.
faq.q: What instruments are carried onboard the DQ satellites?

detail.name: Daqi-1/-2 (DQ-1/-2)

faq.a: DarkCarb is a commercial Earth observation mission developed by SSTL to demonstrate high-resolution MWIR imaging and video from Low Earth Orbit.
faq.q: What is DarkCarb?

faq.a: The mission was designed and built by SSTL, a UK-based small satellite manufacturer and operator established in 1985. SSTL is now a wholly owned subsidiary of Airbus Defence and Space and is well known for pioneering affordable, rapid-deployment small satellites.
faq.q: Who developed DarkCarb?

faq.a: DarkCarb carries a MWIR imager with a cooled Mercury Cadmium Telluride (MCT) detector, capable of 4 m ground sampling distance (GSD) from a 500 km orbit. The instrument can capture both still images and video, allowing observation of rapidly changing events day or night.
faq.q: What is the primary payload onboard DarkCarb?

NASA’s mission DART (Double Asteroid Redirection Test), which was launched on November 24, 2021, was the first-ever mission dedicated to investigating and demonstrating one method of asteroid deflection by changing an asteroid’s motion in space through kinetic impact.

faq.a: Dellingr is operated by NASA and uses the Wallops Flight Facility UHF ground station and Goddard Space Flight Center MOC (Mission Operation Centre).

faq.a: Dellingr is used for measurements of the Earth’s magnetic field and of the composition of the Earth’s atmosphere. Its main purpose, however, was to test processes and technologies for producing efficient, low-cost satellites.
faq.q: What is the use of Dellingr?

faq.a: CubeSats are small and low-cost, and can be used to test new instruments, technologies, and materials for their suitability for integration into more complex space missions.
faq.q: What does the CubeSat do?

faq.a: The Deloitte-1 mission's primary purpose is to provide an on-orbit cyber security testbed to validate Deloitte’s Silent Shield Cyber Intrusion Detection System (IDS), enabling onboard detection and analysis of cyber anomalies in low Earth orbiting satellite systems.
faq.q: What is the primary purpose of the Deloitte-1 satellite mission?

faq.a: The main feature of the Silent Shield IDS is its out-of-band architecture, which is enforced by a hardware-level one-way diode. This design ensures the IDS can monitor data from the satellite bus but is not capable of transmitting any commands back into the spacecraft's software.
faq.q: What is the purpose of the Deloitte-1 Silent Shield payload?

faq.a: Deloitte-1 was successfully launched on March 15, 2025, aboard a SpaceX Falcon 9 rocket as part of the Transporter-13 rideshare mission.
faq.q: When was Deloitte-1 launched?

detail.name: Deloitte-1
detail.summary: The satellite functions as an on-orbit cyber security environment to test and evaluate Deloitte’s Cyber Intrusion Detection System (IDS) against real-world and simulated threats.

faq.a: Diadème-1 (D-1C) and 2 (D-1D) were the second and third satellites in the Diamant-1 (D-1) programme, following Diapason (D-1A). After the success of D-1A, the backup satellite D-1B was not launched, and officials instead decided to proceed directly to D-1C and D-1D to continue the geodetic measurements of D-1A.
faq.q: How do Diadème-1 and 2 relate to CNES’ Diapason (D-1A) satellite?

faq.a: The launch of Diadème-1 was a partial failure as the third stage produced less thrust than expected, resulting in a lower-than-planned orbit. Despite this, the satellite was still able to fulfill its mission.
faq.q: Was the launch of Diadème-1 a failure?

faq.a: Diadème-1 and 2 both carried dual-frequency Doppler transmitters and a retroreflector array for the collection of geodetic measurements, as well as two radio transmitters for the transmission of telemetry data.
faq.q: What instruments were carried by Diadème-1 and 2?

detail.name: Diamant-1 programme - Diapason, Diadème-1, and Diadème-2

faq.a: DICE was launched by NASA(National Aeronautics and Space Administration), but developed by a collaboration between many organisations like Utah State University/Space Dynamics Laboratory (USU/SDL), Astra LLC, and Embry-Riddle Universities, together under the DICE consortium umbrella.

faq.a: DICE satellites measure plasma density distribution and electric fields. This data can convey how SED phenomena form and evolves.
faq.q: What measurements does DICE provide?

faq.a: CubeSat provides many advantages to this mission. The ability to launch multiple satellites in one payload, low-cost access to space for implementation of multi-point measurements. DICE has also demonstrated the development of a high-speed communications link for CubeSats with unprecedented data rates for this class of spacecraft.
faq.q: Why does DICE employ CubeSat form factor?

faq.a: EnMAP is a hyperspectral German satellite, commissioned to take high quality, global photos to track both atmospheric aerosol composition and variation in land vegetation coverage.
faq.q: What is EnMAP used for?

faq.a: EnMAP is a hyperspectral imaging minisatellite, carrying out Earth observation.
faq.q: What type of satellite is EnMAP?

faq.a: EnMAP traverses the Earth on a near-polar orbit, taking images across a wide-range of spectral bands. Information is then transmitted to the ground  using  X-band radio frequency and algorithmically developed into images, using software at the German Research Centre for Geosciences (GFZ).
faq.q: How does EnMAP work?

detail.name: EnMAP (Environmental Monitoring and Analysis Program)
detail.summary: Launched  in April 2022, the mission is managed by the German Aerospace Centre (DLR), with the German Research Centre for Geosciences (GFZ) leading the associated scientific research.

faq.a: EnviSat was an Earth observation mission whose data was used for studying and monitoring the Earth's environment at various scales, from local through regional to global. It was also used for monitoring and management of the Earth's renewable and nonrenewable resources.
faq.q: What was EnviSat used for?

faq.a: EnviSat was in a sun-synchronous polar orbit at an altitude of 800 km and an inclination of 98.55°. The Local Time on Descending Node (LTDN) was at 1000 hours with an orbit period of 100.6 minutes and an exact repeat cycle of 35 days.
faq.q: What orbit was EnviSat in?

faq.a: EnviSat was about 9 metres long and 5 metres wide.
faq.q: How big was EnviSat?

faq.a:
Hyperion is a hyperspectral instrument that enables scientists to understand the chemical constituents of Earth’s surface in fine detail. It is capable of resolving 220 spectral bands from a range of 0.4 to 2.5 μm with a 30m spatial resolution and a swath width of 7.5 km. The continuous spectral coverage in the VNIR and SWIR spectrum is defined by a spectral resolution of 10 nanometers.
The data has been used to identify specific minerals, track vegetation type and vigour of forests and monitor volcanic activity.
faq.q: What imaging does Hyperion do?

faq.a: ERS-1 was launched in July 1991 on an Ariarne-4 launch vehicle. Operations ceased in March 2000 after an onboard attitude control system, where the gyro failed which led to an inability to manoeuvre the aircraft into emergency acquisition mode.
faq.q: When was ERS-1 operational?

faq.a: ERS-1 had the purpose of providing various forms of environmental monitoring data. This includes monitoring land, sea, atmosphere, snow and ice, as well as gravitational and magnetic fields, to provide valuable insights into several disciplines of research, including climate change.
faq.q: What is the purpose of ERS-1?

faq.a: ERS-1 included an Active Microwave Instrument (AMI), a Radar Altimeter-1 (RA-1), an Along-Track Scanning Radiometer and Microwave Sounder-1 (ATSR-1), a Laser Retro-Reflector (LRR) and a Precise Range and Range-Rate Equipment (PRARE) which were used together to generate various different forms of data.
faq.q: What sensors are on board of ERS-1?

faq.a: The key difference between ERS-1 and ERS-2 was in the sensor complement on board the spacecraft. ERS-2 utilised an updated Along-Track Scanning Radiometer and Microwave Sounder (ATSR-2) as well as the addition of a Global Ozone Monitoring Experiment (GOME) which in tandem provided greater data coverage than its predecessor.
faq.q: What were the key differences between ERS-1 and ERS-2?

faq.a: The purpose of ERS-2 was to continue the environmental monitoring across various disciplines which ERS-1 was able to achieve. This included land, sea, atmospheric, snow and ice, vegetation and gravitational and magnetic field data to provide valuable insights into the research of Earth processes, particularly in the field of climate change.
faq.q: What was the purpose of ERS-2?

faq.a: ERS-2 included an Active Microwave Instrument (AMI), a Radar Altimeter-1 (RA-1), an Along-Track Scanning Radiometer and Microwave Sounder-2 (ATSR-2), a Laser Retro-Reflector (LRR), Precise Range and Range-Rate Equipment (PRARE) and a Global Ozone Monitoring Experiment (GOME).

faq.q: How many satellites are in the FY-3 series?

faq.a: GeoCARB would have made daily measurements of the carbon dioxide, carbon monoxide, methane and Solar-Induced Fluorescence quantities in the American atmosphere under all weather conditions, allowing scientists to gain a better understanding of the carbon cycle and to monitor the vegetation health in regions of interest.
faq.q: What would GeoCARB have done?

faq.a: The carbon cycle describes how carbon transfers between different reservoirs located on Earth. This cycle is important for maintaining a stable climate and carbon balance on Earth.
faq.q: What is the role of the carbon cycle?

faq.a: Though the instruments are similar in terms of the technology used to determine the greenhouse gases concentrations, the difference in orbit is crucial. OCO-2 samples a narrow 10 km track over much of the globe on a 16 day repeat cycle, while GeoCarb would have looked at the terrestrial Western Hemisphere continuously from a fixed position, scanning most of this land mass at least once per day.
faq.q: How was GeoCARB different from the mission where its technology was pioneered, OCO-2?

detail.name: GeoCARB (Geostationary Carbon Cycle Observatory)
detail.summary: As the second airborne investigation in NASA’s Earth Venture-Mission series, the mission aimed to measure the concentrations of carbon dioxide, carbon monoxide and methane in the air above South and North America, and to assess vegetation health levels, ultimately advancing the current understanding of Earth's natural exchanges of carbon between land, atmosphere and ocean.

faq.a: GeoEye-1 satellite imagery is used for various applications, including but not limited to defence, disaster response, air and marine transportation, oil and gas exploration, mining production and exploration, mapping of remote regions, location based services, insurance and risk management, and agricultural crop management.
faq.q: What is GeoEye-1 used for?

faq.a: Maxar Technologies currently owns and operates GeoEye-1. GeoEye-1 was originally designed and built by a commercial company named GeoEye, which was merged with DigitalGlobe, which has since been acquired by Maxar.
faq.q: Who owns GeoEye-1?

faq.a: GeoEye-1 is capable of acquiring image data at 0.50 m panchromatic (B&W) and 1.84 m multispectral resolution. It has the ability to locate an object within just three metres of its physical location and features a revisit time of fewer than three days.
faq.q: What does GeoEye-1 do?

detail.name: GeoEye-1 (OrbView-5)
detail.summary: GeoEye-1 imagery is used for various applications, including but not limited to air and marine transportation, defence, disaster response, oil and gas exploration, mining exploration and production, mapping of remote regions, insurance and risk management, location-based services and agricultural crop management.

faq.a: The GEOS mission consisted of three satellites, GEOS-1 (Geodetic Earth Orbiting Satellite-1), GEOS-2 (Geodetic Earth Orbiting Satellite-2), and GEOS-3 (Geodynamic Experimental Ocean Satellite).
faq.q: How many GEOS satellites were there?

faq.a: GEOS-1 was launched on 6 November 1965 from Cape Canaveral, Florida, with a Thor-Delta E launch vehicle and ended operations in January 1968. GEOS-2 was launched on 11 January 1968 from Vandenberg Air Force Base, with a Thor-Delta E1 launch vehicle and ended operations in January 1970. GEOS-3 was launched on 9 April 1975 at Vandenberg Air Force Base with a Delta 1410 launch vehicle and operated until July 1979.
faq.q: When did GEOS satellites operate?

faq.a: Data from the GEOS-1 and GEOS-2 satellites helped to define the structure of the Earth's irregular gravitational field and refine the locations and magnitudes of the large gravity anomalies. GEOS-3 provided the first comprehensive coverage of ocean height over most areas of the world's oceans, providing a better understanding of the ocean geoid.
faq.q: What data did the GEOS missions provide?

detail.name: GEOS (Geodetic Earth Orbiting Satellite) NASA
detail.summary: The mission consisted of three spacecraft: GEOS-1 (Geodetic Earth Orbiting Satellite-1) which operated from November 1965 to January 1968, GEOS-2 (Geodetic Earth Orbiting Satellite-2) which operated from January 1968 to January 1970, and GEOS-3 (Geodynamic Experimental Ocean Satellite) which operated from April 1975 to July 1979.

faq.a: ESA-GEOS 2 was the first geostationary satellite dedicated completely to scientific measurements.
faq.q: What was so significant about ESA-GEOS 2?

faq.a: Magnetic fields, electric fields, plasma interactions with the magnetic field, and electron pitch-angles.
faq.q: What did the ESA-GEOS satellites measure?

faq.a: The launch vehicle experienced a separation problem between stages two and three and as such wasn’t able to obtain a geostationary orbit as planned.
faq.q: Why did ESA-GEOS 1 enter an elliptical orbit?

detail.name: GEOS (GEOstationary Scientific Satellite), ESA

faq.a: Though initially generating classified information for the US Navy, GEOSAT was obtained by NASA and used for precise measurements of the ocean surface, allowing for long-term observations and studies of fronts and eddies, winds, waves, and ocean surface/ice topography. This provided valuable contributions to physical oceanography, geodesy, geophysics, and glaciology.
faq.q: What is GEOSAT used for?

faq.a: GEOSAT was initially operated by the US Navy for its period of life referred to as its Geodetic Mission (GM), where it provided precise oceanic topography data for use by submarine commanders. Later, the satellite was obtained and operated by NASA for its Exact Repeat Mission (ERM), where it was used for long-term global observations of oceanic topography for use in scientific research.
faq.q: Who operated GEOSAT?

faq.a: The GEOSAT spacecraft was able to maintain its orientation so that it was pointing directly towards the ground to within 1⁰ 98% of the time. Its orbit generated a grid of observation of 4 km width
faq.q: How accurate is GEOSAT data?

detail.name: GEOSAT (Geodetic/Geophysical Satellite)

faq.a: The GESat constellation is designed to collect facility-scale data on CH₄ and CO₂ emissions, providing valuable insights for environmental scientists, regulatory bodies enforcing the European Climate Law, and environmental agencies.
faq.q: What is the objective of the GESat Constellation?

faq.a: GESat Gen1 uses a SWIR Fizeau interferometer, a high resolution spectrometer that scans the atmosphere to detect and measure methane emissions. The GESat Gen2 satellite will be equipped with an hyperspectral SWIR instrument capable of detecting both methane and carbon dioxide emissions.
faq.q: How do the GESat Gen1 and GESat Gen2 satellites detect greenhouse gas emissions?

faq.a: GESat Gen2 will feature a next-generation shortwave infrared spectrometer instrument, the HSI SWIR, which features a cryo-cooled Mercury Cadmium Telluride (MCT) sensor. This next-generation technology allows GESat Gen2 to detect both methane and carbon dioxide emissions across a wider range of wavelengths and swaths.
faq.q: How does GESat Gen2 differ from GESat Gen1?

faq.a: GHGSat-D was a high-resolution remote sensing commercial microsatellite that detected and measured greenhouse gases from space. It provided unique emissions imagery and data to businesses, governments, regulators and investors worldwide to optimise their performance and uphold environmental standards.
faq.q: What did GHGSat-D do?

faq.a: Greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions are the sum of emissions of various gases that influence the greenhouse effect, including carbon dioxide (CO2), methane (CH4), nitrous oxide (N2O), and smaller trace gases such as hydrofluorocarbons (HFCs) and sulphur hexafluoride (SF6). GHGSat-D specifically measured carbon dioxide and methane emissions.

faq.a: GHOSt aims to provide enhanced hyperspectral imagery for use in industrial, government, agriculture, environmental and defence sectors. Specific key applications of its use include capturing critical environmental and situational awareness information for defence, detecting minute real-time changes in physical infrastructure, monitoring energy infrastructure and regulatory compliance, detecting environmental erosion and degradation, and monitoring crop yield and agriculture.

faq.a: The GLIMR satellite will most frequently image the Gulf of Mexico. It will also target the United States coastal waters, the Amazon River Plume, as well as regions of interest in the Caribbean Sea, Hawaii and South Pacific waters. Adaptive scheduling will allow GLIMR to target episodic events & hazards.
faq.q: What regions will the GLIMR satellite observe?

faq.a: The GLIMR project has built-in flexibility to adapt as scientists learn how best to use geostationary hyperspectral imagery to benefit science. GLIMR will play an important role in preparing the ocean colour scientific community for future operational geostationary hyperspectral capability such as the GeoXO Ocean Color Instrument (OCX). The data products from GLIMR will also have synergies with Hyperspectral PACE & SBG Missions.
faq.q: Will GLIMR be useful for other Earth observation satellite missions?

faq.a: The GLIMR satellite has the potential for land and atmospheric applications such as measuring land surface reflectance and Air Quality Index. The GLIMR satellite may also be used to validate regional weather/climate models.
faq.q: Will the GLIMR satellite have other multidisciplinary applications?

detail.summary: GLIMR will be the first Hyperspectral ocean colour sensor in the Western Hemisphere to monitor phytoplankton evolution, harmful algal blooms, coral bleaching, and oil spills.

faq.a: Preliminary results of this novel antenna design are very encouraging, and further validation of this technology opens the possibilities of further use of scalable, large aperture meta-lens antennas for other missions. Reduced costs and simplification of satellite bus requirements are the major advantages of this technology.

faq.q: What was the main purpose of GomX-4 satellites?

faq.a: GomX-4 consisted of two satellites, GomX-4A and GomX-4B. They were both launched in February 2018 on board a Long March 2D launch vehicle from Jiuquan Satellite Launch Centre (JSLC) in China.

Agencies responsible for GomX-4B included GomSpace ApS, Technical University of Denmark (TUD) and the European Space Agency (ESA).

detail.summary: Beginning launch in the late 1970s, the constellation consists of a minimum of 24 satellites.

faq.a: GRACE used a precise microwave ranging system to measure the distance between themselves due to gravitational acceleration. GRACE measured Earth's gravity field changes, which were directly related to changes in surface mass.
faq.q: What did the GRACE satellite do?

faq.a: No, GRACE was decommissioned in October 2017 when the satellites ran out of fuel.
faq.q: Is the GRACE satellite still operational?

faq.a: GRACE mission consisted of two identical satellites, GRACE-1 and GRACE-2, that were launched together in March 2002.
faq.q: How many GRACE satellites were there?

detail.name: GRACE (Gravity Recovery And Climate Experiment)
detail.summary: Data from GRACE satellites covered wide application areas.

faq.a: The GRACE satellite series has the objective to measure fluctuations in Earth’s gravitational field, allowing scientists to analyse shifting groundwater, ice sheets, and plate tectonics around the world.
faq.q: What is the objective of the GRACE mission?

faq.a: GRACE-C measures changes in Earth’s gravitational field using two identical satellites flying in close formation. A laser interferometric ranging system measures tiny variations in the distance between the satellites as they orbit Earth, allowing changes in Earth’s mass distribution to be mapped over time.
faq.q: How do the GRACE-C satellites measure changes in Earth’s gravitational field?

faq.a: The GRACE-C mission is a collaborative effort between NASA, DLR (German Aerospace Center) and various other German research centers such as GFZ Helmholtz Centre for Geosciences. The satellites’ primary manufacturer is Airbus Defence and Space and the NASA Jet Propulsion Laboratory.
faq.q: Which organisations are responsible for the GRACE-C mission?

detail.name: GRACE-C (Gravity Recovery and Climate Experiment - Continuity)
detail.summary: GRACE-C is a twin-satellite low Earth orbiting observatory that plans to measure monthly changes in Earth’s gravitational field to improve our understanding of Earth’s water cycle.

name: GRACE-C (Gravity Recovery and Climate Experiment - Continuity)

faq.a: Gravity Recovery and Climate Experiment - Follow On (GRACE-FO) is a successor to the original Gravity Recovery and Climate Experiment (GRACE) mission. GRACE-FO maintains data continuity for measuring changes in high-resolution monthly global models of Earth’s gravity field by tracking water movement and surface mass changes across the planet. Its application area includes monitoring water storage, ice sheets and glaciers, sea level, and solid earth, similar to that of its predecessor, GRACE.
faq.q: What do the GRACE-FO satellites do?

faq.a: As of August 2022, GRACE-FO is still in orbit. GRACE-FO was launched on 22 May 2017 with a minimum mission life planned for 5 years.
faq.q: Are the GRACE-FO satellites still in orbit?

faq.a: There are two identical GRACE-FO satellites.
faq.q: How many GRACE-FO satellites are there?

detail.name: GRACE-FO (Gravity Recovery And Climate Experiment - Follow-On)
detail.summary: GRACE-FO applications include monitoring water storage, ice sheets and glaciers, sea level, and solid earth, similar to that of its predecessor, GRACE.

name: GRACE-FO (Gravity Recovery And Climate Experiment - Follow-On)

faq.a: The programme totals €130 million divided among four axes: Axis 1.1 (€20M thermal satellites), Axis 1.2 (€33M SAR satellites), Axis 2 (€60M optical constellation), and Axis 3 (€17M governmental hub and services). The entire programme is funded through the European Union's Recovery and Resilience Facility (RRF) under Greece's "Greece 2.0" Recovery Plan.
faq.q: What is the total investment in the Greek National Satellite Space Programme and what funds it?

faq.a: The core AXIS programme comprises 13 satellites: four thermal infrared satellites (Axis 1.1), two synthetic aperture radar satellites (Axis 1.2), and seven optical multisensor satellites (Axis 2).
faq.q: How many satellites comprise the Greek AXIS constellation?

faq.a: The first AXIS satellites launched on 28 November 2025, when two ICEYE SAR satellites were successfully deployed into sun-synchronous orbit by SpaceX's Transporter-15 mission launched from Vandenberg Space Force Base. Contract execution is ongoing for the thermal and optical satellite segments, with the governmental hub infrastructure under development by Greek companies and institutions.
faq.q: What was the first satellite in the AXIS programme launched?

faq.a: Frequent coverage is achieved through the use of a constellation of satellites in a low Earth sun-synchronous orbit, which provides short revisit times and large daily coverage. A distributed ground station network enables frequent data downlink.
faq.q: How does the GRUS constellation achieve frequent coverage?

faq.a: GRUS-1 satellites collect high resolution panchromatic and multispectral optical imagery across six different spectral bands while GRUS-3 collects seven different spectral bands. Once corrected, they are suitable for tasks such as land surface mapping and environmental monitoring.
faq.q: What data do the GRUS satellites collect?

faq.a: The first satellite, GRUS-1A, was launched in 2018, followed by an additional four satellites, GRUS-1B, 1C, 1D and 1E, in 2021 to expand the constellation. In 2025, GRUS-3𝛼 was launched to validate the bus and mission payload for the seven GRUS-3 satellites that were launched on July 7, 2026.
faq.q: When were the GRUS satellites launched?

detail.name: GRUS (Axelspace/AxelGlobe)
detail.summary: Five GRUS-1 satellites provide operational imagery, while seven GRUS-3 satellites launched in July 2026 are in initial operations and are expected to increase imaging capacity and revisit frequency for agriculture, forestry, finance, infrastructure and disaster monitoring.

faq.a: The GyeonggiSat programme aims to provide Gyeonggi Province with satellite-derived Earth-observation data to support climate policy, environmental monitoring, disaster response, and land management. GyeonggiSat-1 provides optical imagery, while GyeonggiSat-2A and -2B will add dedicated greenhouse gas sensing capability. The programme is described as the first instance of a South Korean provincial government independently commissioning and funding its own climate satellite system.
faq.q: What is the primary purpose of GyeonggiSat?

faq.a: GyeonggiSat-1 is an enhanced 6U CubeSat with approximate dimensions of 20 cm × 20 cm × 40 cm and a mass of approximately 25 kg, designed and manufactured by Nara Space Technology.
faq.q: What satellite platform is GyeonggiSat-1 based on?

faq.a: GyeonggiSat-1 carries an optical imager designed for land-use monitoring, vegetation dynamics, and disaster damage assessment, while GyeonggiSat-2A and -2B are planned as dedicated greenhouse-gas observation platforms equipped with methane and carbon-dioxide sensors.
faq.q: How does GyeonggiSat-1 differ from the planned GyeonggiSat-2A and -2B satellites?

detail.name: GyeonggiSat (Gyeonggi Climate Satellite Programme)
detail.summary: The programme is designed to deliver satellite-derived environmental data to support provincial climate policy, disaster response, and land-management decisions.

faq.a: The Harmony satellites will orbit in tandem with one of the Sentinel-1 satellites, and they’ll use their receive-only C-band SAR (Synthetic Aperture Radar) to detect C-band radio waves emitted from the SAR of the Sentinel-1 satellite. This will be used to detect small changes in the Earth’s surface.
faq.q: How will the Harmony satellites interact with the Sentinel-1 satellites?

faq.a: Harmony will undertake a sun-synchronous orbit with an altitude of 693 km and an inclination of 98.19°. It has a period of 98.74 minutes and a repeat cycle of 12 days.
faq.q: What orbit will the Harmony satellites undertake?

faq.a: HAWCSat aims to support extreme weather prediction, climate modelling and disaster monitoring, as well as investigating the interactions between atmospheric aerosols, clouds, convection and precipitation.
faq.q: What is the aim of the HAWCSat constellation?

faq.a: The HAWCSat carries two instruments, the Aerosol Limb Imager, and the Spatial Heterodyne Observation of Water instrument.
faq.q: What instruments does HAWCSat carry?

faq.a: The High altitude Aerosols, Water vapour and Clouds (HAWC) programme forms Canada’s part of the NASA-led Atmospheric Observing System (AOS). The HAWC programme includes HAWCSat, which carries the Aerosol Limb Imager (ALI) and the Spatial Heterodyne Observation of Water (SHOW) instrument, as well as the Thin Ice Clouds in Far Infrared (IR) Experiment (TICFIRE) instrument, which will be carried on another AOS mission, AOS-Sky.

detail.name: HAWCSat

faq.a: Hydrosat produces daily Land Surface Temperature (LST) maps at 70 m resolution, captured by a dual-satellite configuration that monitors critical temperature changes for hydrology and agriculture applications.
faq.q: What makes Hydrosat’s thermal imaging capability unique?

faq.a: Data products (LST, evapotranspiration, vegetation indices) are delivered via cloud-optimised GeoTIFFs through APIs, a Python SDK, or a web portal. The system also offers Level 1B radiometrically calibrated imagery with less than 3-hour latency and Level 2A science products featuring emissivity-corrected LST and surface reflectance available within 6 hours of acquisition.
faq.q: How do users access Hydrosat data?

faq.a: The constellation's thermal and multispectral data serve critical applications across multiple sectors. In precision agriculture it enables field-scale irrigation management through crop water stress mapping. Environmental monitoring applications include early drought detection via land surface temperature anomalies and soil moisture analysis, while climate and security uses encompass wildfire risk assessment, urban heat island studies, and monitoring of industrial thermal signatures for regulatory compliance and defence purposes.
faq.q: What applications does Hydrosat support?

faq.a: The nU system is a method for measuring size in CubeSat satellites. Each ‘U’ represents a 10 cm x 10 cm cube, and so, for example, a 1U CubeSat would measure 10 cm x 10 cm x 10 cm, while a 3U CubeSat would measure 10 cm x 10 cm x 30 cm. By having a standardised cross section of 10 cm x 10 cm, CubeSats can be launched from the ISS through the same deployment mechanism.
faq.q: What is meant by 3U?

faq.a: One of the major benefits of CubeSats is their low cost. They are small and often included as secondary payloads on other missions. In addition, their standardised size allows the same launch mechanism from the ISS to be used for different CubeSats. IceCube used off-the-shelf components to create, keeping costs down. Their small size and low cost also make them more accessible to smaller companies and scientific research bases, while also providing shorter development times.
faq.q: What are the benefits of CubeSats?

faq.a: ICESat-2’s ATLAS instrument can take measurements every 70 cm along the satellite’s ground path. In comparison, ICESat’s GLAS instrument took measurements every 170 m. ATLAS is able to measure the slope, or gradient, of terrain in one pass as the ATLAS laser beam is split into six multiple beams compared to ICESat’s GLAS instrument which only used one beam.
faq.q: What are the differences between ICESat and ICESat-2?

faq.a: ICESat-2 has four science objectives: measuring melting ice sheets and investigating how this affects sea level rise, measuring and investigating changes in the mass of ice sheets and glaciers, estimating and studying sea ice thickness, and measuring the height of vegetation in forests and other ecosystems worldwide.

faq.a: The heliosphere is a vast magnetic bubble created by the Sun's solar wind that extends approximately 6 to 9 billion miles from the Sun, encapsulating our entire solar system. It acts as our primary shield against galactic cosmic radiation, which can be harmful to both human explorers in space and technological systems. Understanding its structure, dynamics, and how it varies with solar activity is crucial for space weather forecasting, protecting future human exploration missions beyond low Earth orbit, and understanding how our solar system interacts with the surrounding galaxy.
faq.q: What is the heliosphere and why is it important?

faq.a: IMAP improves on IBEX with approximately 15-35 times greater sensitivity for detecting energetic neutral atoms, improved 4° angular resolution, temporal resolution of 3 months (as opposed to 6), and a ten-instrument payload that simultaneously measures solar wind, energetic particles, magnetic fields, interstellar dust and UV emissions. Located at L1 rather than Earth orbit, IMAP has a superior vantage point for both global heliospheric imaging and real-time space weather monitoring.
faq.q: How does IMAP differ from previous heliosphere missions like IBEX?

faq.a: I-ALiRT is a real-time space weather data broadcasting system that continuously transmits a subset of IMAP's measurements to ground stations worldwide. From IMAP's position at L1, I-ALiRT delivers key solar-wind, energetic-particle and magnetic-field measurements in near real time, typically with <5-minute end-to-end latency and approximately 30 minutes advance warning of solar disturbances. It replaces and extends the ageing ACE real-time capability by providing higher-cadence data and additional parameters vital for protecting astronauts, satellites and ground-based technology.
faq.q: What is the IMAP Active Link for Real-Time (I-ALiRT) and why is it important?

detail.name: IMAP (Interstellar Mapping and Acceleration Probe)
detail.summary: Launched in September 2025, IMAP operates from the Sun-Earth Lagrange point 1 (L1) to map the heliosphere's boundaries and provide real-time space weather data.

faq.a: IM-1, the first mission in Intuitive Machines’ Lunar Payload and Data Services (LPDS) programme, successfully soft-landed a Nova-C lunar lander, named Odysseus, on the Moon on 22 February, 2024. The mission was successful despite some landing gear damage and landing with an unplanned tilt, lasting 14 days.
faq.q: What happened to the first Intuitive Machines mission?

faq.a: IM-2, the second mission in Intuitive Machine’s Lunar Payload and Data Services (LPDS) programme, landed a Nova-C lunar lander, named Athena, on the Moon on 6 March 2025 with an incorrect orientation that prevented the spacecraft from generating sufficient power. The mission ended on 7 March 2025, less than 13 hours after landing. The landing prevented the deployment of certain rovers and while some payloads were able to power on and transmit data, some were not able to become operational.
faq.q: What happened to the second Intuitive Machines mission?

faq.a: Intuitive Machines has been contracted under NASA’s Commercial Lunar Payload Services (CLPS) initiative for four lunar landing missions using the Nova-C lunar lander: IM-1, IM-2, IM-3, and IM-4, to deliver over 20 NASA payloads to the Moon.
faq.q: What is the NASA contract with Intuitive Machines?

faq.a: ECOSTRESS was launched in June 2018 onboard a Falcon 9 launch vehicle, manufactured by SpaceX, from the Kennedy Space Centre in the United States.
faq.q: What are the launch details of ECOSTRESS?

faq.a: As of November 2022, ECOSTRESS is still operational.
faq.q: Is ECOSTRESS still operational?

faq.a: ECOSTRESS is designed to monitor the temperature of soil and plants in order to measure plant evaporation and transpiration (evapotranspiration). From the data collected, we can better understand how much water plants require, how plants react to stress (due to an overabundance or lack of water), and how ecosystems are changing over time.
faq.q: What is the purpose of ECOSTRESS?

detail.name: ISS: ECOSTRESS (ECOsystem Spaceborne Thermal Radiometer Experiment on Space Station)
detail.summary: ECOSTRESS measures plant and soil temperature to improve understanding of plants' necessity for water.

name: ISS: ECOSTRESS (ECOsystem Spaceborne Thermal Radiometer Experiment on Space Station)

faq.a: EMIT was launched to the International Space Centre on July 14, 2022 onboard a SpaceX resupply mission.
faq.q: When was ISS-EMIT launched?

faq.a: ISS-EMIT collects data on the composition of minerals that become airborne dust with the application of determining how dust interacts with Earth’s atmosphere, land, water and organisms. This includes how dust heats and cools the planet and how this may change under future climate scenarios as well as applications in the analysis of the correlation between dust and cloud formation, air quality, human health and blooms of microscopic algae.
faq.q: What is ISS-EMIT used for?

faq.a: EMIT is situated onboard the International Space Station’s (ISS) primary forward-facing hub for externally mounted instruments.
faq.q: Where is ISS-EMIT?

detail.name: ISS: EMIT (Earth surface Mineral dust source InvesTigation)
detail.summary: Hosted onboard the International Space Station  (ISS), the EMIT spectrometer collects data on the composition of minerals that become airborne dust with the aim to expand our knowledge on these fine particles from Earth’s deserts and ultimately how they affect climate.

name: ISS: EMIT (Earth surface Mineral dust source InvesTigation)

faq.a: GEDI is used by NASA to track the vertical profile of Earth’s forests and to study the effect of atmospheric carbon dioxide on the associated biodiversity of these regions.
faq.q: What is GEDI used for?

faq.a: Firstly, to determine the above-ground carbon balance of the land surface. Secondly, ascertain what role the land surface will play in mitigating atmospheric carbon dioxide in the coming decades. Thirdly, to establish how ecosystem structure affects habitat quality and biodiversity.
faq.q: What are the GEDI science objectives?

faq.a: GEDI produces the vertical profile of Earth’s forests by measuring the reflected LIDAR waveforms off the surface of the planet. A machine learning algorithm is trained to correlate pixel brightness with canopy height.
faq.q: What does GEDI measure?

detail.name: ISS: GEDI (Global Ecosystems Dynamics Investigation Lidar)
detail.summary: GEDI operations were paused in March 2023, and resumed nominal science operations with all three lasers on June 11, 2024.

name: ISS: GEDI (Global Ecosystems Dynamics Investigation Lidar)

faq.a: Jason-2 was used to monitor ocean topography. This included measuring ocean circulation, the rise in sea level caused by global warming, the ocean’s role in Earth’s climate and improving global forecast and climate predictions.
faq.q: What was Jason-2 (OSTM) used for?

faq.a: Jason-2 (OTSM) was launched in June 2008, on a Delta launch vehicle from Vandenberg Air Force Base (VAFB), United States.
faq.q: When was Jason-2 launched?

faq.a: There are three Jason satellites: Jason-1, Jason-2 and Jason-3. Only Jason-3 is operational as of August 2022.
faq.q: How many Jason satellites are there?

detail.name: Jason-2/OSTM
detail.summary: Jason-2 successfully obtained a continuous record of observations in line with previous missions which included measurements of time-averaged ocean circulation, global sea-level change and improved open ocean tide models, until it was decommissioned in October 2019.

faq.a: Jason-3 aims to provide continuity to Poseidon/TOPEX, Jason-1 and Jason-2 missions which support applications related to extreme weather events, operational oceanography, climate and forecasting.
faq.q: What is the purpose of Jason-3?

faq.a: Yes, the Jason-3 mission has been extended past its three year nominal lifetime and remains in orbit and operational as of August 2022.
faq.q: Is Jason-3 still in orbit?

faq.a: Jason-3 was developed and led collaboratively by NOAA (National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration), EUMETSAT (European Organization for the Exploitation of Meteorological Satellites), CNES (Centre national d'études spatiales) and NASA. It was launched in January 2016 onboard a SpaceX Falcon 9 v1.1 vehicle from Vandenberg Air Force Base in California.

faq.a: KaleidEO is a fully owned subsidiary of the company SatSure. KaleidEO develops Earth observation payloads and will operate a planned constellation of microsatellites, expanding SatSure’s geospatial data capabilities.
faq.q: What is KaleidEO and how is it linked to SatSure?

faq.a: KaleidEO is developing a constellation of four microsatellites, each weighing around 175 kg. These satellites will be equipped with optical multispectral imagers and onboard edge processing systems that pre-process data before transmission to ground stations.
faq.q: What types of satellites is KaleidEO building?

faq.a: KaleidEO adopts a demand-led, vertically integrated model. It designs its systems to close specific performance gaps in Earth observation, tailoring solutions for diverse users, including commercial clients, government bodies, and research institutions.
faq.q: What is unique about KaleidEO’s approach to Earth observation?

detail.name: KaleidEO Constellation (SatSure)

faq.a: Kanopus-V 1 recorded multispectral and panchromatic imagery in the Visible and Near-Infrared ranges to monitor Earth’s surface, the atmosphere, ionosphere, and magnetosphere to detect and study the probability of strong Earthquake occurrence and other natural disasters.

KhalifaSat is also part of a broader UAE objective to strengthen the standing of the Arab region in the competitive field of space science.
faq.q: What is the purpose of KhalifaSat?

faq.a: KOMPSAT-3 was designed to meet Korea’s need for high-resolution optical imagery in the form of Geographical Information Systems that highlight environmental, agricultural and oceanographic monitoring.
faq.q: What is the purpose of KOMPSAT-3?

faq.a: KOMPSAT-3 is an observation mission of the Korea Aerospace Research Institute (KARI) that is funded by the Korean Ministry of Education, Science and Technology (MEST).
faq.q: Which organisation owns KOMPSAT-3?

faq.a: There are a total of five bands, one panchromatic band (450nm - 900 nm), one blue multispectral band (450 nm - 520 nm), one green multispectral band (520 nm - 600 nm), one red multispectral band (630 nm - 690 nm) and one near-infrared (NIR) band (760 nm - 900 nm).
faq.q: How many bands does the Advanced Electronic Image Scanning System (AEISS) on KOMPSAT-3 have?

faq.q: How many modes does the Corea SAR Instrument (COSI) on KOMPSAT-5 contain?

faq.a: KOMPSAT-6 aims to expedite the production of high resolution, spaceborne SAR (Synthetic Aperture Radar) imagery, providing continuity for KOMPSAT-5.
faq.q: What is the purpose of KOMPSAT-6?

faq.a: KOMPSAT-6 has been developed by, is owned by and will be operated by KARI (Korea Aerospace Research Institute), a Korean Government organisation.
faq.q: Who owns KOMPSAT-6?

faq.a: KOMPSAT-6 carries two instruments, XSAR (X-band Synthetic Aperture Radar), a high spatial resolution multi-purpose radar imager, and S-AIS (Satellite- Automatic Identification System), a wireless system that identifies the position, bearing, heading, destination and cargo of large vessels.
faq.q: What instruments does KOMPSAT-6 carry?

detail.name: KOMPSAT-6 (Korea Multi-Purpose Satellite-6) / Arirang-6
detail.summary: KOMPSAT-6 aims to provide high resolution radar imagery required in GIS (Geographical Information Systems), disaster & environment monitoring and ocean & land management.

faq.a: KOMPSAT-7 aims to provide continuity to the Korean Multipurpose Satellite (KOMPSAT) programme by serving national demand for high resolution optical imagery over the Korean Peninsula.
faq.q: What is the purpose of KOMPSAT-7?

faq.a: KOMPSAT-7 provides continuity to KOMPSAT-3A’s observations with the High Resolution Advanced Electronic Image Scanning System (AEISS-HR), an updated model of KOMPSAT-3’s visible and near-infrared (VNIR) multispectral imager. Additionally, the satellite features an Advanced Infrared Module, capable of thermal signature detection, through day or night.
faq.q: What instruments are aboard KOMPSAT-7?

faq.a: KOMPSAT-7 was successfully launched by Arianespace on December 1, 2025 from Kourou, French into Sun-synchronous low-Earth orbit (LEO).
faq.q: When did KOMPSAT-7 launch?

Launched in December 2025, the Korean Multipurpose Satellite-7 (KOMPSAT-7), or Arirang 7, is an optical imaging satellite designed by the Korean Aerospace Research Institute (KARI) to support national applications in high resolution land and vegetation monitoring.

detail.name: Kondor-E (Kondor-Experimental SAR Spacecraft)
detail.summary:
Launch 2

After the failure of Kondor-E, the replacement satellite Kondor-E1 was launched on 19 December, 2014 on a Russian Strela rocket from silo 59 at Baikonur Cosmodrome’s site 175 at 04:43 UTC.

faq.a: Kondor-FKA, where FKA stands for Federal Space Agency, is a Russian hydrology and military reconnaissance satellite developed by NPO Mashinostroyeniya (NPOM) for ROSCOSMOS.
faq.q: What is Kondor-FKA?

faq.a: Kondor-FKA houses the Synthetic Aperture Radar-10 (SAR-10) radar imaging payload, which will support measurements of soil moisture, biomass, fractions of land vegetation and sea ice, and land cover and topography. This instrument can provide high-resolution imagery of ground features as small as 1 m in size.
faq.q: What instruments are onboard Kondor-FKA?

faq.a: “Kondor” refers to satellites built by and for the Russian military. The civilian Kondor-E stands for data export of soil and hydrology. While Kondor-FKA does hold hydrology capability, it is the military counterpart of the Kondor series, whose data is encrypted and maintained only for Russian military use.
faq.q: How does Kondor-FKA differ from the Kondor-E satellite?

detail.name: Kondor-FKA

faq.a: LAGEOS acts as a permanent reference point. Being small, high density, and homogenous in design, in addition to having a highly stable orbit, the satellites can provide information about the Earth and universal systems via unexpected orbital deviations. For example, the data obtained have been used to measure movements in the Earth’s tectonic plates, gravitational phenomena, and irregularities in the rotation of the Earth.
faq.q: What does LAGEOS do?

faq.a: Satellite Laser Ranging (SLR) is a system whereby several laser-ranging stations are positioned at various locations around the globe to form a network. Each station can fire lasers at specific satellites that are designed to reflect laser pulses back to their source and time how long it takes the laser pulses to return to the station. These timings are highly precise, and when several stations fire simultaneously at such a satellite, its location relative to the Earth can be accurately determined. This method is so precise that positions can be determined down to within a few millimetres, as in the case of the LAGEOS satellites.
faq.q: What is SLR in geodesy?

faq.a: LAGEOS stands for LAser GEOdynamics Satellite.
faq.q: What does LAGEOS stand for?

faq.a: LIBRA, CLARREO and TRUTHS all offer SI traceability. The main difference between these missions is the instruments; LIBRA will have 4 instruments on board, whereas CLARREO and TRUTHS have 2 and 3 respectively. The instruments involved in each mission fall into four categories: instruments to measure spectrally resolved infrared radiance, spectrally resolved reflectance of solar radiation, total solar irradiance, and spectrally resolved solar irradiance. Although the instruments are similar, they differ in spectral coverage, spectral resolution, and measurement uncertainty.
faq.q: How does LIBRA differ from CLARREO and TRUTHS?

faq.a: Monthly lunar observations are major components of the on-orbit calibration strategies of LIBRA. The reference and target satellite observe the same lunar phase which is realised by attitude manoeuvres each month. Then, the intercalibration can be performed using a standard lunar radiation model, such as RObotic Lunar Observatory (ROLO).
faq.q: How will LIBRA use lunar observations for intercalibration?

faq.a: No, a prototype of the LIBRA satellite has not been built. The prototypes of the payloads have been built, including an absolute radiance standard IR calibrator, a RSB self-correction absolute calibrator based on Spontaneous Parametric Down-Conversion (SPDC), and a cavity-type absolute cryogenic radiometer (ACR) with 20 K operational temperature.

faq.q: Who owns MetOp-SG?
