faq.a: As of July 2022, MEV-1 is currently servicing Intelsat 901 and MEV-2 is servicing Intelsat 10-02. They are intended to perform the satellite life extension service for a period of five years, beginning in August 2020 and April 2021 respectively.
faq.q: What satellites are MEV-1 and MEV-2 currently servicing?

faq.a: MEV-1 and MEV-2 have a similar design but have contrasting service characteristics. MEV-1 controls the orbit of the customer satellite whereas MEV-2 acts as a new engine and fuel tank to extend the client spacecraft’s life.
faq.q: What is the difference between MEV-1 and MEV-2?

faq.a: MEV-1 was launched in October 2019 by ILS (International Launch Services) and MEV-2 was launched in August 2020 by Ariane Space.
faq.q: When were MEV-1 and MEV-2 launched?

detail.name: MEV-1 & 2 (Mission Extension Vehicle-1 and -2)
detail.summary: MEV-1 provides life-extending services by taking over the propulsion and attitude control functions to steer, point and relocate the client spacecraft, while MEV-2 acts as fuel for a functioning satellite without means of significant propulsion.

faq.a: MicroCarb will aim to monitor the fluxes of carbon dioxide at the surface between the atmosphere and the oceans and vegetation with high precision. This data will be used for a number of purposes including increasing our understanding of the functioning of vegetation, identifying and quantifying the terrestrial ecosystem carbon sinks and sources at yearly scales, quantifying oceanic carbon sources and sinks at a yearly scale and their responses to announced climate changes, and contributing to the measurement of carbon emission linked to fossil fuels use.
faq.q: What will MicroCarb be used for?

faq.a: MicroCarb is being developed by the French Space Agency, Centre National d’Etudes Spatiales (CNES), with support from the United Kingdom Space Agency (UKSA).
faq.q: Who is developing MicroCarb?

detail.name: MicroCarb (Carbon Dioxide Monitoring Mission)
detail.summary: Considered a successor to other carbon dioxide missions such as Greenhouse gas Observing Satellite (GOSAT) and Orbiting Carbon Observatory-2 (OCO-2), the objective of MicroCarb is to monitor the fluxes of carbon dioxide between the atmosphere, oceans and vegetation.

faq.a: While the best accuracy of the Equivalence Principle achieved by experiments on Earth is 10-13, the MicroSCOPE mission aimed to improve this value by two orders of magnitude, up to 10-15.
faq.q: What was the target accuracy of the MicroSCOPE mission?

faq.a: The satellite employed a micro propulsion system to precisely cancel these factors.
faq.q: How did MicroSCOPE counteract external perturbations, such as atmospheric drag, radiation pressure or electromagnetic forces?

faq.a: In the inertial mode, fep was about 1.7 x 10-4 Hz, while in the rotating mode, it reached 1.0 x 10-3 Hz
faq.q: What value did the Equivalence Principle frequency have throughout the experiment?

detail.name: MicroSCOPE (Micro-Satellite à traînée Compensée pour l'Observation du Principe d'Equivalence)
detail.summary: Between its launch in April 2016 and its end of life in October 2018, the mission successfully tested the WEP to an accuracy of the order of 10-14.

name: MicroSCOPE (Micro-Satellite à traînée Compensée pour l'Observation du Principe d'Equivalence)

faq.a: MOS-1 was launched on 19 February 1987, and MOS-1B was launched on 7 February 1990, both from Tanegashima, Japan.
faq.q: When were the MOS-1 Satellites launched?

faq.a: The MOS Constellation consisted of MOS-1 and 1B, Japan’s first marine observation satellites. They aimed to provide effective utilisation of natural resources and environmental protection.
faq.q: What was the objective of the MOS Constellation?

faq.a: MOS (Marine Observation Satellite), also referred to as Momo (meaning “Peach tree”), was a pair of satellites which were Japan’s first marine observation satellites.
faq.q: What does MOS stand for?

detail.summary: MOS-1 and 1B launched in February 1987 and February 1990, and finished their operations in November and April 1996, respectively.

faq.a: The Meteosat Third Generation (MTG) program consists of six satellites, two sounders and four imagers. The first satellite, MTG-I1, was launched on December 13 2022 on board the Ariane-5 rocket.
faq.q: When will Meteosat Third Generation launch?

faq.a: Sentinel-4 is a part of the Copernicus European Earth observation program and has the primary objective of observing the diurnal cycle of the tropospheric composition over Europe and North Africa. Sentinel-4 will be hosted on board the MTG-S satellites. MTG will continue data from previous satellites for weather forecasting and predictions of severe weather events.
faq.q: What is the difference between Sentinel-4 mission and MTG mission?

faq.a: The Meteosat Third Generation (MTG) mission consists of two different types of satellites, a sounder (MTG-S) and an Imager (MTG-I).  MTG-I will carry the Flexible Combined Imager (FCI) and Lightning Imager (LI), while MTG-S will carry the Infrared Sounder (IRS) as well as the instrument for the Copernicus Sentinel-4 mission, the UV-visible- near infrared imaging spectrometer (UVN).
faq.q: What is the difference between MTG-S and MTG-I?

detail.name: MTG (Meteosat Third Generation)
detail.summary: There will be a total of six satellites launched, consisting of four MTG-I (Imager) satellites and two MTG-S (Sounder) satellites, with the first MTG-I satellite launched on 13 December 2022.

faq.a: MULA is currently being developed by the Advanced Satellite Development and Know-How Transfer for the Philippines (ASP) Project of the Space Technology Applications, Mastery, Innovation, and Advancement (STAMINA4Space) Program and the Philippine Space Agency (PhilSA), in conjunction with industry support from Surrey Satellite Technology Ltd.
faq.q: Who owns MULA?

faq.a: MULA aims to capture medium resolution optical imagery, with applications predominantly in agriculture, forestry and mapping, as well as advancing the Philippines national space program.
faq.q: What is the aim of MULA?

faq.a: MULA will carry the Surrey Satellite Technology Ltd. (SSTL) TrueColour Imager, providing multispectral optical imagery with a wide swath and medium spatial resolution.
faq.q: What instrument does MULA carry?

detail.name: MULA (Multispectral Unit for Land Assessment)
detail.summary: The mission aims to produce medium resolution multispectral optical imagery for primarily agricultural applications, as well as advance the Filipino national space program.

faq.a: The MuSat satellites are designed, built, and operated by Muon Space, a American commercial aerospace company specialising in Earth observation constellations through a vertically integrated platform.
faq.q: Who operates the MuSat satellites?

faq.a: MuSat satellites support climate monitoring, national security, and resource management by providing high-resolution Earth observation data. Their modular design allows them to host diverse payloads, including thermal infrared sensors and GNSS reflectometry instruments for environmental and defense applications.
faq.q: What are the main applications of the MuSat satellites?

faq.a: MuSat-1, the first satellite developed by Muon Space, was launched on June 12, 2023, aboard the SpaceX Transporter-8 rideshare mission. It successfully fulfilled its mission objectives before re-entering Earth’s atmosphere on December 11, 2024.
faq.q: When did the first MuSat satellite launch?

detail.name: MuSat (Muon Space)
detail.summary: MuSat-1 launched on June 12, 2023.

faq.a: NACHOS-1 decayed on February 3, 2023 after almost one year in orbit. NACHOS-2 re-entered Earth’s atmosphere on June 27, 2024.
faq.q: Is NACHOS-1 still operational?

faq.a: Due to its size and efficiency, NACHOS was a more cost-effective way of measuring harmful trace gases.
faq.q: Why was NACHOS so important for climate change?

faq.a: NACHOS-1 was to verify instrument design and gather enough test data to ensure the technology concept is feasible.
faq.q: Why was NACHOS-1 in orbit for only a year?

detail.name: NACHOS (Nano-satellite Atmospheric Chemistry Hyperspectral Observation System)
detail.summary: NACHOS used newly developed, computationally efficient algorithms that could  rapidly extract gas signatures from massive hyperspectral datasets, taking just minutes to do so on the onboard computer.

name: NACHOS (Nano-satellite Atmospheric Chemistry Hyperspectral Observation System)

faq.a: Roman's mission is to explore dark energy, exoplanets, and infrared astrophysics through wide-area sky surveys and direct imaging.
faq.q: What is the Roman Space Telescope's primary mission?

faq.a: Roman has the same mirror size as Hubble but a field of view 200 times larger, allowing for more extensive sky surveys in less time.
faq.q: How does Roman compare to Hubble?

faq.a: Roman carries two primary instruments, the Wide Field Instrument (WFI) and the Coronagraph Instrument.
faq.q: What instruments are onboard Nancy Grace Roman Telescope?

detail.name: Nancy Grace Roman Telescope
detail.summary: The mission aims to expand our understanding of the universe through unprecedented wide-field surveys and high-contrast imaging.

faq.a: NanoMagSat is occasionally referred to as Swarm Delta as it follows Swarm Alpha, Bravo, and Charlie.
faq.q: Does NanoMagSat go by any other names?

faq.a: The British company, Open Cosmos, is operating the mission which is largely funded by ESA. The scientific lead is the Université Paris Cité’s Institut de Physique du Globe de Paris (Paris University’s Institute of Physics), and there is a collection of smaller institutions developing the payloads.
faq.q: Who is developing NanoMagSat?

faq.a: NanoMagSat will monitor Earth’s magnetic field and ionosphere, as a follow up to ESA’s Swarm mission.
faq.q: What will NanoMagSat do?

detail.name: NanoMagSat
detail.summary: NanoMagSat will monitor Earth’s magnetic field and ionosphere over its expected lifetime of three years.

faq.a: This mission primarily measures the intensity of Earth’s Magnetic Field at the South Atlantic Anomaly (SAA) region and on the Brazilian sector of Ionosphere Equatorial Electrojet (EEJ)
faq.q: What does NanosatC-Br1 measure?

faq.a: EEJ and SAA cause variations in the magnetic field strength and can nausea upsets for satellite navigation. They are important phenomena to consider for air-traffic control as well.
faq.q: What are the advantages of studying EEJ and SAA?

faq.a: The sensors onboard the satellite are based on the Hall effect, providing precise measurements with a resolution of up to 15 nT.
faq.q: How does NanoSatC-Br1 measure the magnetic field?

detail.name: NanoSatC-Br1(Brazilian CubeSat Project-1)
detail.summary: It has been semi-operational since 2016, due to batteries no longer holding the minimum voltage required, and only transmits beacons in sunlight.

faq.a: The NarSha constellation is designed to monitor methane emissions globally, mainly identifying sources of methane in cities and providing near-real-time, high-resolution data for climate change analysis.
faq.q: What is the primary purpose of the NarSha constellation?

faq.a: NarSha satellites are equipped with a hyperspectral imager that operates in the short-wave infrared (SWIR) range. Methane absorbs light at specific wavelengths in this range, allowing the imager to detect and quantify methane plumes as small as 100 kg/h with high precision.
faq.q: How does NarSha detect methane emissions?

faq.a: Nara Space utilises its in-house developed Nara Space Technology On-Board Computer (NST OBC) for its satellites. This is an Atmel ARM-based computer specifically designed for CubeSats, capable of handling high data volumes and transfer rates.
faq.q: What technology does Nara Space use for its satellite’s on-board computing and data handling?

detail.name: NarSha Constellation / Korea Methane Monitoring Microsatellite (K3M)
detail.summary: Its primary objective is to create a full-scale global constellation for monitoring methane emissions and identifying their sources.

name: NarSha Constellation / Korea Methane Monitoring Microsatellite (K3M)

detail.name: NEA Scout (Near Earth Asteroid Scout)
detail.summary: The secondary payloads, 13 CubeSats, were selected through a series of announcements of flight opportunities, a NASA challenge and negotiations with NASA’s international partners.

faq.a: The NeuSAR mission is a pathfinder mission for a potential constellation of small SAR satellites.
faq.q: What is the aim of the NeuSAR mission?

faq.a: NeuSAR was designed and developed by DSO National Laboratories, Singapore, with support from the Office for Space Technology and Industry (OSTIn).
faq.q: Who owns NeuSAR?

faq.a: NeuSAR was successfully launched on June 30, 2022 aboard an ISRO Indian Polar Satellite Launch Vehicle at 0832 EDT.
faq.q: When was NeuSAR launched?

detail.name: NeuSAR
detail.summary: NeuSAR is the world’s first fully polarimetric small satellite SAR.

faq.a: NewAthena will be an open X-ray observatory, aiming at a wide range of astrophysical investigations of celestial systems such as a complete a census of black hole growth, processes that occur outside the event horizons of black holes to power active galactic nuclei and the formation of hot gas large-scale structures.
faq.q: What is the aim of NewAthena?

faq.a: NewAthena carries the Wide Field Imager (WFI), an array of silicone active pixel sensors with an integrated detector/amplifier structure, and the X-ray Integral Field Unit (X-IFU), an X-ray spectrometer consisting of a microcalorimeter array.
faq.q: What instruments does NewAthena carry?

faq.a: NewAthena will be situated at the First Lagrange point, L1, a gravitationally semi-stable point approximately 1,500,000 km from Earth.
faq.q: Where will NewAthena operate from?

detail.name: NewAthena (Advanced Telescope for High Energy Astrophysics)
detail.summary: In particular, NewAthena will investigate the origins and formation of hot gas large-scale structures, as well as the influence of supermassive black holes in active galactic nuclei on the cosmological evolution of galaxies.

name: NewAthena (Advanced Telescope for High Energy Astrophysics)

faq.a: Satellogic has the overall goal to grow its Aleph-1 Constellation to 300 satellites, which would allow global coverage with a revisit time of 5 minutes.
faq.q: How many NewSat microsatellites does Satellogic aim to have in the Aleph-1 Constellation?

faq.a: SpaceX is Satellogic’s preferred vendor for rideshare missions, with a 2022 agreement reserving a launch capacity for the company’s next 68 satellites.
faq.q: Who launches NewSat satellites?

faq.a: Since 2018, Satellogic has respected its tradition of naming the satellites after remarkable women in STEM, commemorating the brilliant pioneers who made significant contributions and discoveries that impacted and inspired millions of lives around the world.
faq.q: Why are the NewSat microsatellites nicknamed after women?

detail.name: NewSat (Aleph-1 Constellation)
detail.summary: The first launch was in May 2016.

faq.a: NigeriaSat-2 was launched August 17, 2011 aboard a Dnepr-1 launch vehicle from the Yasny/Dombarovsky launch site in Russia.
faq.q: When was NigeriaSat-2 launched?

faq.a: NigeriaSat-2 has the primary objective of providing high resolution optical imagery of the Earth’s surface. Its data has applications in supporting food security and agricultural data collection, mapping and security, as well as the provision of data continuity for the NigeriaSat-1 system.
faq.q: What does NigeriaSat-2 do?

faq.a: NigeriaSat-2 was designed and developed by Surrey Satellite Technology Ltd., but is owned and operated by the Nigerian government organisation NASRDA (National Space Research and Development Agency).

faq.a: NigeriaSat-X undertakes a sun-synchronous near-circular orbit with an altitude of 700 km, an inclination of 98°, and a period of 97 minutes.
faq.q: What orbit does NigeriaSat-X undertake?

faq.a: NigeriaSat-X was launched in August 2011 aboard a Russian-made Dnepr-1 launch vehicle at the Dombarovsky Air Base in Russia.
faq.q: What were the launch details of NigeriSat-X?

faq.a: Whilst NigeriaSat-X does play an important role in monitoring land and vegetation, it was largely built as an exercise to develop the skills of Nigerian engineers. They were guided in the construction by Surrey Satellite Technology Ltd (SSTL).

detail.name: NigeriaSat-X
detail.summary: The satellite aims to assist in the fields of disaster management, agriculture monitoring, and security.

faq.a: Nimbus-7 served as a stabilised platform for the testing of advanced systems for sensing and collecting data in the atmospheric, oceanographic and meteorological disciplines from 1978 until 1994.
faq.q: What did Nimbus-7 do?

faq.a: Nimbus-7 was the last of the Nimbus satellites, however many satellites can trace their heritage to experiments from the Nimbus satellites. These include but are not limited to: Aura, Aqua, Terra, NOAA TIROS/POES,  Landsat, IceSat, SeaSat, and Suomi NPP.
faq.q: What satellites succeeded from Nimbus-7?

faq.a: The Nimbus series was a series of seven satellites operated by the National Aerospace and Space Administration (NASA) first launched in October 1964. The Nimbus satellites were the first three-axis stabilised meteorological satellites which tested many experiments for collecting data in the atmospheric, oceanographic and meteorological disciplines until 1994.
faq.q: What is the Nimbus series?

detail.name: Nimbus-7
detail.summary: Nimbus-7 served as a stabilised platform for the testing of advanced systems for sensing and collecting data in atmospheric, oceanographic and meteorological disciplines until October 1994.

faq.a: The pointing accuracy of NISAR is so accurate that it can detect changes in land, snow, and ice cover as little as 4 mm per year.

faq.a: NorSat-1 and -2 are owned by the Norwegian Space Centre (NSC), with contributions from the University of Toronto Institute for Aerospace Studies (UTIAS) Space Flight Laboratory (SFL), PMOD (Physikalisch-Meteorologisches Observatorium Davos) World Radiation Centre, and the University of Oslo.
faq.q: Who developed NorSat-1 and -2?

faq.q: What is the aim of NorSat-1 and -2?

faq.a: Both Norsat-1 and -2 are operational, but are expected to leave service in December 2022.
faq.q: Are NorSat-1 and -2 still operational?

detail.summary: NorSat-2 is the second installation of the microsatellite series, also launched in July 2017, and aims to provide additional, advanced ship tracking and Very High Frequency (VHF) Data Exchange (VDE) services.

faq.a: The NORSAT-3 microsatellite was launched as a passenger payload on 29 April 2021 on a Vega launch vehicle operated by Arianespace, which lifted off successfully from the Guiana Space Center, Europe’s Spaceport in French Guiana.
faq.q: How was NORSAT-3 launched?

faq.a: NORSAT-3 aids the Norwegian Coastal Administration in understanding the maritime situation on Norway’s seas by receiving AIS and radar signals from vessels.
faq.q: What is NORSAT-3 used for?

faq.a: An important new addition on NORSAT-4 will be a low-light imaging camera.
faq.q: How will NORSAT-4 differ from NORSAT-3?

detail.name: NORSAT-3 and 4

faq.a: NorSat-TD is aimed as a technological demonstration mission onboard a microsatellite form factor to validate and test new payloads and concepts from a variety of agencies and companies from Europe.
faq.q: What is the purpose of NorSat-TD?

faq.a: Space Flight Laboratory (SFL) of University of Toronto, Institute for Aerospace Studies (UTIAS) in Canada was awarded the contract to develop NorSat-TD from the Norwegian Space Agency.
faq.q: Who developed NorSat-TD?

faq.a: NorSat-TD will be carrying an Automatic Identification System (AIS) receiver, Small Communication Active Terminal (SmallCAT), VHF (Very high Frequency) Data Exchange System (VDES), Onboard Laser Reflector and Satellite Collision Avoidance.

detail.summary: NorSat-TD is designed as a microsatellite aimed at validating and testing out new payloads and concepts from Norway, the Netherlands, France and Italy.

faq.a: NovaSAR-1 is a technology demonstration satellite mission designed to test the capabilities of a new low cost S-band SAR (Synthetic Aperture Radar) platform.
faq.q: What is NovaSAR-1?

faq.a: NovaSAR-1 uses S-band SAR to image Earth through all weather conditions, including heavy cloud and smoke, and during the night. This offers a valuable data advantage for many applications including forest monitoring, ship detection and disaster management.
faq.q: What is the imaging advantage of NovaSAR-1?

faq.a: NovaSAR-1 is currently the only operational NovaSAR satellite, however, there are plans to develop other SAR imaging satellites. The target constellation is three satellites to provide more frequent revisit times.

faq.a: The initial launch contains five satellites, with continuous installments of five until a total of 20 satellites are operational.
faq.q: How many satellites are to be launched in NUVIEW’s constellation?

faq.a: NUVIEW sets out to map the Earth for detailed infrastructure/environment planning. Lidar improves upon both optical and SAR imaging through an increase in accuracy (35 times that of optical imagery and 120 times that of SAR) and is thus the best sensor type for this task. Additionally, lidar is an active sensing technique, projecting photons and then receiving the reflected photons to map continuously as it is not daylight dependent.
faq.q: Why is NUVIEW developing a lidar satellite constellation?

faq.a: The US Department of Defence has funded NUVIEW through the National Security Innovation Capital (NSIC). Across two approvals, $7.75 million has been provided by the NSIC in the name of high quality 3D mapping and production of geospatial data.
faq.q: Which US government department provided funding to NUVIEW?

detail.name: NUVIEW Constellation
detail.summary: With support from the US Department of Defence, NUVIEW aims to increase the quantity of topographical data available for infrastructure planning and environmental management.

faq.a: OceanSat-2 was developed by the Indian Space Research Organization (ISRO) and launched on a Polar Satellite Launch Vehicle (PSLV) from Satish Dhawan Space Centre (SDSC) on the east coast of India.
faq.q: Who launched OceanSat-2?

faq.a: OceanSat-2 provides service continuity for operational users of Ocean Color Monitor (OCM) flown on OceanSat-1. It provides data for the study of surface wind, ocean surface strata, observation of chlorophyll concentrations, monitoring phytoplankton blooms, study of atmospheric aerosols and suspended sediments in the water.
faq.q: What is OceanSat-2 used for?

faq.a: OceanSat-2 was launched on 24 September 2009.
faq.q: When was OceanSat-2 launched?

detail.name: OceanSat-2
detail.summary: Other application areas include the study of surface wind, ocean surface strata, observation of chlorophyll concentrations, monitoring phytoplankton blooms, and study of atmospheric aerosols and suspended sediments in the water.

faq.a: Oceansat-3 is owned and operated by the India Space research Organisation (ISRO), although it does carry the ARGOS-4 data collection system, which has been supplied by the National Centre for Space Studies (CNES) of France.
faq.q: Who owns Oceansat-3?

faq.a: Oceansat-3 aims to provide service continuity for users of Oceansat-2 data, as well as improve upon existing remote sensing capabilities in the field of oceanography.
faq.q: What is the aim of Oceansat-3?

faq.a: Oceansat-3 carries four instruments, the Oceansat Scatterometer (OSCAT-3), The Ocean Colour Monitor (OCM-3), a Sea Surface Temperature Monitor (SSTM) and the ARGOS-4 data collection system, also known as the Advanced Data Collection System (ADCS-4).
faq.q: What instruments does Oceansat-3 carry?

detail.name: Oceansat-3
detail.summary: It aims to provide service continuity for users of Oceansat-2 data, as well as improve upon existing remote sensing capabilities in the field of oceanography.

faq.a: OCO-2 is owned and operated by NASA (National Aeronautics and Space Administration) of the USA.
faq.q: Who owns OCO-2?

faq.a: OCO-2 was launched in July 2014, after the loss of its predecessor, OCO-1, in a launch vehicle failure on February 24, 2009.
faq.q: When was OCO-2 launched?

faq.a: The Forest satellites were OroraTech’s initial technology demonstrators with limited revisit times (up to 14 days), while the Wildfire constellation is a network of 8+ satellites providing near-continuous global wildfire monitoring with revisit times as short as twice daily, aiming for 30-minute intervals. Both use similar thermal infrared sensors, but the constellation leverages coordinated orbits and inter-satellite communication for real-time alerts and broader coverage.
faq.q: What is the difference between OroraTech’s Wildfire constellation and the Forest satellites?

faq.a: The constellation offers real-time wildfire alerts within minutes by using advanced AI onboard processing and inter-satellite links, enabling continuous 24/7 monitoring, including critical afternoon and nighttime periods when fires are most active. This is a significant improvement over earlier satellites, which had longer revisit times and could not provide timely alerts during these key periods.
faq.q: How does the Wildfire constellation improve wildfire detection compared to previous satellites?

faq.a: The Phase 1 satellites from OroraTech operate in a circular low Earth orbit at 550 km altitude with a 97° inclination, designed for continuous global coverage and optimised detection of wildfire hotspots, including night-time monitoring.
faq.q: What orbits do the OroraTech Wildfire satellites operate in?

detail.name: OroraTech Wildfire Constellation
detail.summary: Launched in March 2025 and built in collaboration with Spire Global, the mission consists of eight 8U CubeSats to provide continuous thermal infrared imaging of global fire activity, supporting disaster response.

faq.a: The cartwheel formation ensures consistent spatial baselines for effective single-pass interferometry, enhancing data accuracy and temporal resolution.

faq.a: The Hongtu-1 constellation provides competitive capabilities with high-resolution imagery and accurate terrain mapping, enabled by its unique multi-static configuration. Its ability to perform single-pass interferometry and improve DEMs with meter-scale accuracy makes it comparable to advanced SAR systems like RADARSAT-2 and Sentinel-1.

faq.q: Who developed Pleiades Neo?

faq.a: Pléiades Neo improves upon its predecessors, the Pléiades, in areas such as twice the revisit capacity, a larger ground resolution and more than double the daily acquisition capacity. The Pléiades, however, have a larger swath width which can increase the scale of its remote sensing applications.

Otherwise, data from Pleiades can be acquired at a cost. Through their Third Party Missions scheme, the European Space Agency (ESA) also offers access to Pleiades data for users from ESA member states and European Commission Member States.
faq.q: Is Pleiades imagery free?

faq.a: The Pleiades constellation provides a very high spatial resolution of 0.7 m for panchromatic image bands and 2.8 m for multispectral image bands.
faq.q: What is the resolution of Pleiades imagery?

faq.a: The bilateral agreement between the heads of government of Italy and France is referred to as ORFEO (Optical and Radar Federated Earth Observation). Its objective is to provide a dual high-resolution Earth observation system through the two-satellite constellation, Pleiades, under French leadership, and the four-satellite constellation, COSMO-SkyMed, under Italian leadership. These constellations observe in the optical and microwave region respectively to provide a long-term perspective on a number of high-quality data products and services on the commercial market. This data has applications in cartography, agriculture, forestry, hydrology, geological prospecting and defence.
faq.q: What is the purpose of ORFEO?

faq.a: Unlike visible or infrared radiometers, PolSIR is able to probe and penetrate ice clouds to analyse ice cloud structure.
faq.q: Why are sub-millimeter/microwave wavelengths used by PolSIR advantageous?

faq.a: PolSIR will increase our understanding of ice clouds in tropic/sub-tropic regions, leading to better understanding of precipitation, especially for tropical storms and cyclones. Additionally, better understanding patterns of ice clouds helps models of the atmosphere understand how different species of ice cloud contribute to Earth’s albedo and the refraction of radiation.
faq.q: What will PolSIR measure?

faq.a: PolSIR measures between the nominal range of 680-325 GHz to fill a gap in previously corrected data. PolSIR’s radiometer uses the frequency of 325GHz for its role in penetrating deep and sensing large snow ice, and 680GHz which is a matured version of the technology used by IceCube (883GHz).
faq.q: What frequencies does PolSIR observe?

faq.a: PROBA-V Companion CubeSat is used to monitor worldwide vegetation classification, crop monitoring, famine prediction, food security, disaster monitoring and biosphere studies, and it will also be used in the scientific fields of Geology and Epidemiology, as well as in infrastructure and urban applications.
faq.q: Is PROBA-V Companion CubeSat only used to monitor vegetation growth?

faq.a: PROBA-V Companion CubeSat will fly a cut-down version of the vegetation-monitoring instrument aboard the Earth-observing satellite PROBA-V to perform experimental combined observations with its predecessor.

faq.a: ESA decided to combine the PROBA-V Companion CubeSat and PROBA-V missions to conduct studies on BRDF (Bidirectional reflectance distribution function) effects thanks to different observation geometry. Harmonisation of datasets captured by different platforms, in a different orbit, but with the same sensor, and also calibration / validation for small satellites.
faq.q: Why did ESA decide to combine PROBA-V Companion CubeSat and PROBA-V missions?

faq.a: The mission length of Radar In a Cubesat (RaInCube) was only two and a half years as it was a technology demonstration mission and had no fuel onboard to sustain its orbit. It successfully demonstrated its technology after one month of life and the rest of its time in orbit was a bonus.

faq.a: Radar In a Cubesat (RaInCube) was launched to the International Space Station onboard the uncrewed Cygnus OA-9E Commercial Resupply Services mission on May 21, 2018. RaInCube was then deployed from the ISS on July 13, 2018.

faq.q: Was RaInCube a successful mission?

faq.a: Both Space Weather Next satellites, L1-A and L1-B, will operate from Lagrange Point 1, where the gravitational attractions of the Earth and Sun allow it to remain stable in space. They will be complemented by ESA’s Vigil spacecraft, operating from Lagrange Point 5.

faq.a: Scintillations are rapid fluctuations in the phase and amplitude of a wave, and in this context are caused by irregularities in ionospheric electron density in the Earth’s atmosphere. faq.a: Swarm is measuring the Earth’s magnetic field strength and direction, as well as the forces of solar radiation and wind experienced by satellites in Low Earth Orbit (LEO).
faq.q: What is Swarm measuring?

faq.a: Swarm-A and Swarm-C travel in the same polar orbit parallel to one another, whereas Swarm-B travels in a slightly higher and less inclined orbit.
faq.q: What are the differences between the orbits of Swarm?

faq.a: One notable discovery made in 2022 from Swarm data was that at the outer edge of the core, there is a magnetic field that oscillates every seven years and is travelling westward at 1500 km/year.
faq.q: What has Swarm discovered?

faq.a: The constellation will consist of three satellites.

faq.a: The primary aim of the SWIRSAT mission is to reduce the costs of carbon emission data acquisition. Its data also has wider applications in precision agriculture and disaster monitoring.
faq.q: What is the aim of SWIRSAT?

faq.a: The mission consists of two instruments: Chameleon SWIR and MultiScape200. Chameleon SWIR is a nadir scanning multispectral radiometer that will measure CH4 and CO2 atmospheric column mole fractions. MultiScape200 is a high resolution optical payload that will provide high resolution imagery base maps.
faq.q: What instruments will SWIRSAT carry?

faq.a: Designed to make the first-ever global survey of Earth's surface water, the Surface Water and Ocean Topography (SWOT) satellite will collect detailed measurements of how water bodies on Earth change over time.

faq.a: SWOT is a collaboration between NASA’s Jet Propulsion Laboratory (JPL) and the French Space Agency (Centre National d'Etudes Spatiales, CNES), with contributions from the Canadian Space Agency (CSA) and the United Kingdom Space Agency (UKSA).
faq.q: Which countries collaborated with NASA for the SWOT mission?

faq.a: SWOT makes use of KaRIn, DORIS, GPSP, LRA and AMR-S
faq.q: What instruments does SWOT employ?

detail.name: SWOT (Surface Water Ocean Topography)
detail.summary: The data collected by these surveys could improve ocean circulation models and weather predictions, while aiding in freshwater management around the world.

faq.a: Terra uses five onboard instruments to provide extensive data on Earth’s atmosphere, land surface, ocean and cryosphere. Within the atmosphere it takes measurements on cloud properties, radiative energy flux, tropospheric chemistry, aerosol properties, atmospheric temperature and humidity. On the land surface it provides data on land cover and land use change, vegetation dynamics, surface temperature, fire occurrence and volcanic effects. In the ocean and cryosphere it can measure temperature, phytoplankton and dissolved organic matter, sea ice, snow cover and land ice change.
faq.q: What is the purpose of Terra?

faq.a: Launched in December 1999, Terra had an original design life of six years. However, its operation has been extended, with a predicted end-of-life in September 2023 (as of July 2022).
faq.q: Is Terra still working?

faq.a: Terra was developed by NASA as a part of their multi-national ESE (Earth Science Enterprise) program between the US, Japan and Canada. These countries each contributed the mission by developing one or more of the sensors onboard.
faq.q: Who developed Terra?

detail.name: Terra (EOS/AM-1)
detail.summary: With an original design life of six years, the operation has been extended to continue to provide an extensive variety of valuable data on Earth’s atmosphere, land surface, ocean and cryosphere.

faq.a: THEMIS was launched to help understand magnetospheric substorms (disturbances in Earth's magnetic field). Data collected by THEMIS probes have been used to determine when and where substorms begin, how the individual components of the substorm interact, and how local disruptions link with global substorm phenomena.
faq.q: What is the purpose of THEMIS?

faq.a: fter THEMIS successfully completed its primary mission in 2010, the two outer probes were sent into orbit around the Moon, and formed a new mission named ARTEMIS (Acceleration, Reconnection, Turbulence and Electrodynamics of the Moon’s Interaction with the Sun). These satellites systematically measure conditions in the  magnetotail — the distant tail of Earth’s magnetic environment. ARTEMIS also collects data on the lunar space environment and the Moon’s surface composition, magnetic field and core structure.
faq.q: What is the ARTEMIS mission?

faq.a: All five THEMIS spacecraft were launched as a secondary payload on the Delta-2-7925-10 vehicle on 17 February, 2007.  The launch site was CCAS (Cape Canaveral Air Station), Florida.
faq.q: When was THEMIS launched?

detail.name: THEMIS (Time History of Events and Macroscale Interactions during Substorms)
detail.summary: In 2010, the two outer probes were redirected into a Lunar orbit to form a new mission named ARTEMIS (Acceleration, Reconnection, Turbulence, and Electrodynamics of the Moon's Interaction with the Sun) and collect information about the space environment of the moon.

name: THEMIS (Time History of Events and Macroscale Interactions during Substorms)

faq.a: TIMED is a NASA mission aimed at deepening the understanding of the interaction between the sun and the upper region of the Earth’s atmosphere.

faq.a: By dividing the responsibility of instrument operation to each principal investigator, it allows the freedom of control for each instrument to take measurements as needed and operate without necessitating approvals for each variation.
faq.q: Why does TIMED have multiple centres for its operation?

faq.a: Due to the extensions of TIMED, it was able to observe an entire 11-year solar cycle, providing insight into longer solar trends like cooling and contraction of the upper atmosphere due to climate change.

faq.a: TDRS was developed to accommodate the needs of a growing fleet of NASA-operated satellites, as the original system of ground stations provided coverage for only approximately 15% of a satellite’s orbit. faq.a: The Triton mission was originally named FormoSat-7R, and was intended as an independent thirteenth satellite in the planned Constellation Observing System for Meteorology, Ionosphere, and Climate 2 (COSMIC-2), known as FormoSat-7, before the constellation was subsequently reduced to Triton and six FormoSat-7 satellites.
faq.q: Is Triton also named FormoSat-7R?

faq.a: Triton has a 82% domestic production rate, with Taiwanese company Aerospace Industrial Development Corporation (AIDC) leading the satellite integration of components supplied by Taiwanese companies such as Fongshen Technology and the Syscom Computer. The satellite was constructed by UK Aerospace company Surrey Satellite Technology Limited (SSTL).
faq.q: Was Triton fully developed in Taiwan?

faq.a: Triton carries a Global Navigation Satellite System Reflectometry (GNSS-R) payload, which is a space grade GPS receiver. It receives signals reflected from the Earth’s surface in low Earth orbit (LEO) emitted by GNSS constellations from which it derives surface parameters for use in typhoon and weather predictions, and research.
faq.q: What instruments are onboard Triton?

detail.name: Triton (FormoSat-7R)
detail.summary: Equipped with a Global Navigation Satellite System Reflectometry (GNSS-R) receiver, it uses reflected GNSS signals to derive Earth surface characteristics for applications in weather forecasting, disaster management, and climate research.

faq.a: The Tropical Rainfall Measuring Mission (TRMM) was a research satellite that operated between 1997 and 2015. It hosted five sensors onboard that monitored and gathered data about precipitation and latent heating within the tropics and subtropics, orbiting the Earth every 93 minutes.
faq.q: What does TRMM do?

faq.a: TRMM had several instruments onboard which collected many different datasets about precipitation in the Tropics. TRMM instruments include cloud profile and rain radars, imaging multispectral radiometers and lightning sensors, which produce datasets including 3D rainfall distribution over land and oceans, rain rate, sea surface temperatures, wind speeds, cloud types, and lightning flash data.
faq.q: What is TRMM data?

faq.a: The goal of the TRMM satellite was to monitor and study precipitation and latent heating over the global Tropics. The data collected is used to increase our understanding of Earth’s global energy and water cycles and contribute to better modelling and predictions of Earth’s climate system.
faq.q: What is the goal of the TRMM satellite and its instruments?

detail.name: TRMM (Tropical Rainfall Measuring Mission)
detail.summary: As part of the NASA Earth Science Enterprise (ESE) program, TRMM hosted a variety of instruments that gathered information about precipitation and latent heating between the Tropics of Capricorn and Cancer, to further our understanding of global energy, water cycles and climate.

name: TRMM (Tropical Rainfall Measuring Mission)
taxonomy: Cloud type, amount and cloud top temperature

faq.a: TROPICS aims to measure temperature, humidity, and precipitation so that we may better understand the evolution and structure of storms.
faq.q: What does TROPICS aim to do?

faq.a: Twilight conditions will be limited by orbit selection. Calibration generally takes place during the Earth shadow part of the orbit, allowing the systems to be well-protected from solar and other radiation damage. For example, spectral channels can be calibrated at a rate of at least one wavelength per orbit on the Earth shadow side of the orbit.
faq.q: What will TRUTHS scanning do while in twilight conditions and in Earth’s shadow?

faq.a: Using the Hyperspectral Imaging Spectrometer, TRUTHS will radiometrically calibrate the moon allowing it to be used as a true SI transfer standard. This would allow historic missions to be re-calibrated and Fundamental Climate Data Records (FCDRs) to be extended backwards in time with greater accuracy, since many sensors have been observing the moon.
faq.q: Will TRUTHS perform lunar calibrations?

faq.a:
The relatively unique non-sun-synchronous orbit of TRUTHS, whilst providing many observational benefits, presents significant additional challenges to both the spacecraft and payload. Varying thermal and solar viewing (power) conditions adds complexity to the mission.
To account for this, calibrations of the Hyperspectral Imaging Spectrometer will be performed under a variety of simulated and real orbit conditions to assess any variances due to inadequate thermal stabilities.
faq.q: What are some challenges TRUTHS is predicted to face?

name: TRUTHS (Traceable Radiometry Underpinning Terrestrial-and Helio-Studies)

faq.a: TSX uses an X-band SAR sensor for scientific applications in the fields of hydrology, geology, climatology, oceanography and disaster monitoring systems.
faq.q: What is TerraSAR-X (TSX) used for?

faq.a: As of June 2022, TSX is still operational. TSX has completed its expected service of five years but remains in a condition that is still functional. The satellite is expected to serve several more years before deorbiting.
faq.q: Is TerraSAR-X still operational?

faq.a: Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) works via transmitting microwave signals and then receiving the microwave signals that have returned. The SAR instrument functions by measuring the time taken to receive a signal back to the Earth's surface, and uses this to calculate distance.
faq.q: How does synthetic aperture radar work on TSX?

faq.a: The planned applications of TSX-NG SAR data included the creation of DEMs (Digital Elevation Models), monitoring critical infrastructure, and situational awareness and maritime applications. However, no data will be obtained by the TSX-NG mission as it has been cancelled.
faq.q: What will TSX-NG SAR (Synthetic Aperture Radar) data be used for?

faq.a: The objective of Van Allen Probes was to study the Van Allen Radiation belts and their effect on the spacecrafts electronics and materials in orbit.
faq.q: What did Van Allen Probes do?

faq.a: The provision of two probes enabled simultaneous two-point measurements by identical spacecraft. This helped determine if an event occurred throughout the belts or travelled across the belts.
faq.q: Why are there two Van Allen Probes?

faq.a: Van Allen probes were launched in August 2012 from Cape Canaveral, USA.
faq.q: When did Van Allen Probes launch?

detail.name: Van Allen Probes
detail.summary: The two probes were launched in August 2012 and decommissioned in 2019.

faq.a: The Veery constellation aims to provide low-cost, high frequency and global radio scatterometer observations of ocean wind speed and direction, soil moisture content and sea ice coverage.
faq.q: What is the aim of the Veery constellation?

faq.a: There are 15 pathfinder or technology demonstration Veery satellites, however, none carry the compact scatterometer to conduct the intended measurements, with the final constellation planned to consist of 8-12 satellites.
faq.q: How many Veery satellites are there?

faq.a: The Veery scatterometer will have a spatial resolution of 25 km for individual satellites, but can be processed to 6 km resolution using supplementary imagery from other satellites in the constellation.
faq.q: What is the spatial resolution of the Veery compact scatterometer?

faq.q: What are the WildFireSat orbit details?

faq.a: The WorldView Legion Constellation is owned and operated by the US-based commercial company Maxar Space Systems (formerly DigitalGlobe).
faq.q: Who operates WorldView Legion?

faq.a: WorldView Legion is planned to consist of six high resolution optical satellites. The first pair were launched together on May 2, 2024.
faq.q: How many satellites are in the WorldView Legion Constellation?

faq.a: WorldView Legion aims to provide high resolution and frequency monitoring and mapping services to customers to realise actionable insights for defence and intelligence, maritime, and infrastructure applications.
faq.q: What applications does WorldView Legion have?

detail.name: WorldView Legion
detail.summary: The first two satellites of the constellation were launched together in May 2024.

faq.a: The aim of WorldView-1 is to provide high spatial resolution imagery of the Earth’s surface for commercial, defence and intelligence applications.
faq.q: What is the purpose of WorldView-1?

faq.a: WorldView-1 has outlasted its 7.25 year design life, which expired in December 2015, and remains operational as of July 2022.
faq.q: Is WorldView-1 still operating?

faq.a: WorldView-1 was developed by and is owned and operated by Maxar Technologies Inc.
faq.q: Who owns WorldView-1?

detail.name: WorldView-1
detail.summary: WV-1 is the successor to QuickBird-2 and aims to provide high spatial resolution imagery of the Earth’s surface to defence, intelligence and commercial industries.

faq.a: As of July 2022, WorldView-2 is currently operational, having outlasted its expected lifetime of 7.25 years, which expired in January 2017.
faq.q: Is WorldView-2 still operating?

faq.a: WorldView-2 is owned and operated by Maxar Technologies Inc., a commercial entity specialising in high spatial resolution imagery. The WorldView series was previously owned by DigitalGlobe, before the company merged with MDA to form Maxar Technologies in February 2017.
faq.q: Who owns WorldView-2?

faq.a: WorldView-2 imagery is available by direct purchase from Maxar Technologies Inc., the owner and operator of the WorldView series.
faq.q: How can I access WorldView-2 imagery?

detail.name: WorldView-2
detail.summary: It is the successor to WorldView-1 (WV-1) and aims to meet the increasing demand for high spatial resolution imagery.

faq.a: There are 29 total spectral bands including a panchromatic band, eight multispectral bands, 8 SWIR (shortwave infrared) bands and 12 CAVIS (Cloud, Atmosphere, Vapours, Ice, and Snow) bands.
faq.q: How many bands are there in WorldView-3?

faq.a:
WorldView-3 collects high-resolution imagery, which is assisted by the CAVIS bands of the imager. The objective of CAVIS is to monitor the atmosphere and provide correction data to improve WorldView-3's high-resolution imagery when it images Earth objects through haze, soot, dust or other obscurants.
WorldView-3 uses Control Moment Gyroscopes developed by BATC (Ball Aerospace & Technologies Corporation), which reorient a satellite over a desired collection area in 4-5 seconds, compared to the 30-45 seconds needed for traditional reaction wheels.
faq.q: How does WorldView-3 work?

faq.a: WorldView Legion is the next evolution of Maxar’s industry leading WorldView constellation. WorldView Legion is a fleet of high-performance satellites that dramatically expands the ability to revisit the most rapidly changing areas on Earth to better inform critical, time-sensitive decisions. There will be six satellites in the fleet, with launch planned for 2022.
faq.q: What is WorldView Legion?

detail.name: WorldView-3
detail.summary: WorldView-3 added to DigitalGlobe’s existing constellation of QuickBird, WorldView-1 and WorldView-2, with WorldView-4 joining the constellation in 2016.

faq.a: WorldView-4 had the capabilities of providing high-resolution images and film of Earth to businesses and governments.
faq.q: What did WorldView-4 do?

faq.a: WorldView-4, due to its altitude of 617 km, had a flight velocity of 7.5 km/s. This allowed the satellite to observe 680,000 km2 of Earth’s surface per day. However, with the entire fleet of Maxar imagery satellites, 60% of Earth's surface would have been observed monthly.
faq.q: How fast was WorldView-4?

faq.a: WorldView-4 became inactive after the gyroscopes that controlled the rotation of the satellite failed. Without control of where the instrument was pointed, images are no longer able to be obtained.
faq.q: Is WorldView-4 still active?

detail.name: WorldView-4
detail.summary: WorldView-4 went out of action in January 2019, after its orientation was unable to be controlled.

faq.a: XCUBE is operated by Xplore, a US-based commercial space company focused on providing advanced remote sensing data. The mission supports Xplore's vision to build the XCRAFT satellite constellation.
faq.q: Who operates XCUBE?

faq.a: XCUBE was successfully launched into orbit on December 21, 2024, at 3:34 a.m. PST onboard a SpaceX Falcon 9 rocket, as a part of SpaceX’s Bandwagon-2 rideshare mission.
faq.q: When was XCUBE launched?

faq.a: XCUBE carries the Simera HyperScape100 hyperspectral camera, delivering a spatial resolution of 4.2 - 4.8 m per pixel, with a 19.4 km swath width, using 1 nm spectral increments across the 442 - 884 nm wavelength range.
faq.q: What is XCUBE's imaging resolution and coverage?

detail.name: XCUBE
detail.summary: The satellite was launched on 21 December 2024 and is designed to capture hyperspectral images for applications in Earth Observation (EO), space domain awareness, maritime surveillance, and astronomy.

detail.name: Xona Space Systems
detail.summary: Orbit: Sun-synchronous orbit with an altitude of 525 km and an inclination of ~97º.

faq.a: XPoSat aims to study various dynamics of bright X-ray sources in extreme conditions, including the emission mechanisms of black holes and pulsars.
faq.q: What is the aim of XPoSat?

faq.a: The satellite carries the X-ray Spectroscopy and Timing (XSPECT), and the Polarimeter Instrument in X-rays (POLIX).
faq.q: What instruments does XPoSat carry?

faq.a: The satellite was successfully launched aboard ISRO’s PSLV-C58 mission on January 1, 2024.
faq.q: When was XPoSat launched?

detail.name: XPoSAT
detail.summary: XPoSat was successfully launched on January 1, 2024.

faq.a: The first phase of the YunYao Aerospace Constellation includes 90 LEO nano-satellites, all part of the YunYao-1 series.
faq.q: How many satellites are included in the first phase of the YunYao Aerospace Constellation?

faq.a: YunYao-1 satellites carry the YunYao Receiver, which combines GNSS-R and GNSS-RO instruments into a single unit. It collects atmospheric and surface data using signals from GNSS constellations. The GNSS-RO component is used to gather temperature, humidity, and electron density profiles.
faq.q: What instruments are carried aboard YunYao-1 satellites?

faq.a: The GNSS-RO measurements are highly accurate, with an absolute mean bias of less than 1.5%, making the data suitable for weather forecasting and scientific research. Accuracy is highest between altitudes of 4 km and 40 km. The precision can vary at lower altitudes due to factors such as atmospheric moisture and signal interference.
faq.q: How accurate are GNSS-RO measurements from YunYao satellites?

detail.name: Yunyao Aerospace Constellation
detail.summary: This is achieved using Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS) Radio Occultation (GNSS-RO) and GNSS Reflectometry (GNSS-R) instruments.

name: Yunyao Aerospace Constellation
taxonomy: Soil moisture in the roots region
